name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage for the catering website that surfaces key event catering categories, quick entry points into core flows (browse packages, build a custom menu, check availability, request a quote), and featured packages, using only dynamic data from backend interfaces.

core_features: Load homepage content (intro text, primary categories, featured packages, quick actions) via getHomePageData and use it to populate the main interactive elements.

Load top-level package categories for site-wide navigation via getPackageCategoriesForNavigation and expose them as navigation links into the packages listing page with the appropriate categoryId URL parameter.
Provide a primary category overview section where each dynamically returned category can be clicked to navigate to packages.html with categoryId set to the category’s code.

Provide a quick-actions section that maps dynamically returned quickActions to navigations such as browsing category-specific packages, launching the custom menu builder, opening the availability page, or opening the generic quote request page.
Provide a featured packages section where each package card is built from dynamic Package data and navigates to package_detail.html with the package id in the URL.
Provide global header navigation, including a Home link (no parameters) and a cart icon that navigates to cart.html.
user_workflows:
Browse packages for a specific event type:
1) User lands on Home (no required URL parameters). The page initializes by calling getHomePageData and getPackageCategoriesForNavigation.
2) The primary category overview and quick actions are populated with categories from getHomePageData.primaryCategories and navigation categories from getPackageCategoriesForNavigation.
3) User clicks on a category item in the category overview or a corresponding quick action (e.g., a corporate, wedding, party, or coffee-break related action).
4) The click handler reads the category’s code from the bound PackageCategory entity and navigates the browser to packages.html with ?categoryId=<category.code>.
5) The user then continues their flow on packages.html filtered by that category.

Browse all packages (not limited to a single category):
1) User lands on Home and data is loaded via getHomePageData.
2) User activates a quick action or a dedicated control whose quickActions entry has a categoryCode representing the "all events" pseudo-category.
3) The click handler reads this categoryCode and navigates to packages.html?categoryId=<quickAction.categoryCode>.
4) The packages listing page then shows all packages according to that categoryId value.

Start a custom dinner menu builder flow:
1) User lands on Home and quickActions is loaded from getHomePageData.
2) User clicks a quick action whose type is "custom_menu_builder".
3) The click handler constructs a URL for custom_menu.html and passes the mealType URL parameter (such as the value defined by the backend for this action) as ?mealType=<derivedMealType>. If the quick action does not explicitly include a mealType, the handler uses the interface contract for this action (e.g., defaulting to the dinner-oriented builder when appropriate).
4) The browser navigates to custom_menu.html with the mealType query parameter, so the custom_menu page can initialize its state accordingly.

Check date availability for an event:
1) User lands on Home; quickActions is loaded.
2) User clicks a quick action whose type is "check_availability".
3) The click handler navigates to availability.html without adding extra URL parameters from Home.
4) The availability page, once loaded, uses its own logic to show a calendar and slots based on its own interfaces.

Submit a generic quote request not tied to a specific package:
1) User lands on Home; quickActions is loaded.
2) User chooses a generic quote-related quick action whose type is "request_quote" (not associated with a particular package).
3) The click handler navigates to quote_request.html without any package-related URL parameters, indicating that the user is starting a general quote request.
4) The quote_request page then lets the user specify event details and package preferences there.

View details for a highlighted package from Home:
1) User lands on Home; getHomePageData returns a featuredPackages array.
2) The featured package list component renders one interactive card per featuredPackages entry, binding each to the underlying Package.id.
3) User clicks a featured package card.
4) The click handler reads the bound package id and navigates to package_detail.html?id=<package.id>.
5) The package detail page uses the id URL parameter to load full package information.

Use the main navigation to reach other package categories or the cart:
1) Home page initializes a navigation menu using getPackageCategoriesForNavigation.
2) User clicks a navigation item associated with a specific category.
3) The click handler reads that category’s code and navigates to packages.html?categoryId=<category.code>.
4) At any point, the user can click the cart icon in the header; the click handler navigates directly to cart.html with no additional parameters.
interactions: On page load (DOMContentLoaded or equivalent), the home-data-fetcher component invokes getHomePageData and getPackageCategoriesForNavigation sequentially or in parallel. Successful responses are passed down to the other components via props or a shared state model.

When a user clicks on a primary category card in the service-category-grid component, the onClick handler retrieves the associated PackageCategory.code from the bound data and performs a client-side navigation to packages.html?categoryId=<code>.

When a user clicks on a quick action in the quick-action-panel component:
- If quickAction.type is "browse_corporate_packages", "browse_wedding_packages", "browse_party_packages", or "browse_coffee_breaks", the handler reads quickAction.categoryCode and navigates to packages.html?categoryId=<quickAction.categoryCode>.
- If quickAction.type is "custom_menu_builder", the handler navigates to custom_menu.html with a mealType query parameter derived from the QuickAction specification (e.g., custom_menu.html?mealType=<derivedMealType>).
- If quickAction.type is "check_availability", the handler navigates to availability.html.
- If quickAction.type is "request_quote", the handler navigates to quote_request.html.
When a user clicks on a featured package card in the featured-package-list component, the onClick handler reads the bound Package.id and navigates to package_detail.html?id=<package.id>.
When a user clicks on a category link in the main-navigation-menu component, the onClick handler reads the PackageCategory.code and navigates to packages.html?categoryId=<code>.
When a user clicks the Home link in the main-navigation-menu, the onClick handler navigates to index.html without any query parameters, resetting the user to the default home state.
When a user clicks the cart icon in the cart-entry-point component (generally displayed in the header), the onClick handler navigates to cart.html without any query parameters.

If any data-fetching interface fails (e.g., getHomePageData or getPackageCategoriesForNavigation), the home-data-fetcher component exposes an error state that other components can observe to conditionally render fallback messages or minimal navigation links (for example, a Home link and basic navigation stubs) without hardcoding any specific data content.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Home page does not require or consume any incoming URL parameters for its own data loading; it always calls getHomePageData and getPackageCategoriesForNavigation on load to obtain current dynamic content. The page state is therefore independent of the URL query string for this route. All stateful navigation out of Home is controlled solely through URL parameters passed to other pages:
- When navigating to packages.html, the page always includes a categoryId query parameter derived from either a selected PackageCategory.code or a quickAction.categoryCode (e.g., packages.html?categoryId=<code>). This ensures that the Packages Listing page can initialize its PackageFilterState.categoryFilter based on the URL parameter.
- When navigating to custom_menu.html from a quick action of type "custom_menu_builder", the URL includes a mealType parameter (e.g., custom_menu.html?mealType=<mealTypeValue>) computed from the quick action configuration. This allows the Custom Menu page to initialize its CustomMenu.mealType and related filter logic.
- When navigating to availability.html, quote_request.html, or cart.html from Home, no additional parameters are set by this page. Those pages are responsible for their own state initialization based on their specific requirements and URL parameters.
Any transient UI states on Home (such as loading, loaded, or error states) are handled purely in memory during the session and are not synchronized to the URL, because the specification for this page defines no incoming query parameters.

components.functionality: Central initialization component that triggers all required data fetches for the Home page and exposes the resulting data and loading states to child components.
Builds the primary site navigation for package categories based on the data returned by getPackageCategoriesForNavigation and provides links for Home and category-specific package browsing.
Displays the main heading and supporting text for the Home page using heroTitle and heroSubtitle from getHomePageData, serving as a concise explanation of what users can accomplish from this page.

Renders an interactive list or grid of primary categories for common event types using the primaryCategories array from getHomePageData; each item can be clicked to navigate to the packages listing with the appropriate categoryId.

Displays the quickActions entries from getHomePageData as clickable buttons or links that route users into core flows such as category-specific package browsing, custom menu creation, checking availability, or requesting a quote.

Shows a list of featured packages based on featuredPackages from getHomePageData, including dynamic information such as the package name, per-person display price, associated category label, and any tagline provided. Each item is clickable to navigate to the package_detail page for that specific package.
Provides a single interactive element (e.g., cart icon or link) that navigates the user from Home to cart.html without modifying or inspecting cart contents on this page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a constrained max-width content area centered in the viewport. The left text block occupies roughly 4 columns and the right form block around 8 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Replicate the mockup’s structure with a full-width, fixed header containing centered navigation, sitting above a spacious main content band on a white background. The first main band uses a two-column layout: on the left, a concise homepage introduction (page-intro-summary); on the right, a vertically stacked interaction block that mimics the form column (quick-action-panel). Below this hero band, surface data-driven content—service-category-grid followed by featured-package-list—in a linear, top-to-bottom flow within the same centered, constrained content area.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Replicate the mockup’s structure with a full-width, fixed header containing centered navigation, sitting above a spacious main content band on a white background. The first main band uses a two-column layout: on the left, a concise homepage introduction (page-intro-summary); on the right, a vertically stacked interaction block that mimics the form column (quick-action-panel). Below this hero band, surface data-driven content—service-category-grid followed by featured-package-list—in a linear, top-to-bottom flow within the same centered, constrained content area.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate the top 15–20% of the viewport to the full-width header housing the main-navigation-menu and cart-entry-point. The remaining vertical space is dedicated to the main content, starting with a generous 64px gap below the header. Inside the main content, maintain a 12-column grid: page-intro-summary occupying roughly 4 columns on the left and quick-action-panel occupying about 8 columns on the right. Subsequent sections (service-category-grid and featured-package-list) span the full content width inside the max-width container, with 48–64px vertical spacing between them to preserve the airy, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a calm, moderately spacious density. In the header, navigation items are spaced horizontally with 16–24px between them and comfortable vertical padding. In the hero band, the left intro text is minimal and uses short paragraphs; the right quick-action-panel stacks its items with consistent 16–24px vertical gaps, echoing form-field spacing from the mockup. The service-category-grid and featured-package-list use generous card padding and regular gutters, avoiding overcrowding while still presenting several items above the fold.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the colored header and navigation (with a subtle cue for the active Home state) down to the left-aligned page-intro-summary heading, then naturally across and into the right-side quick-action-panel, which functions like the dominant interactive column in the mockup. From there, the eye continues downward into the service-category-grid and then the featured-package-list, each forming clear horizontal bands. The strong vertical stacking within quick-action-panel and within the grids reinforces a simple, top-down, left-to-right reading path.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a fixed, full-width header bar spanning the top of the viewport, in a muted brand color, with navigation centered in the middle and a discrete cart entry point aligned to the far right. Below this, a wide white content band stretches across the page but is visually constrained by a centered max-width container, creating a calm editorial frame. Within this main area, the first section uses a two-column layout: on the left, a compact block of text introduces the catering services; on the right, a larger card-like quick action panel mirrors the proportions of a form column, stacking key actions such as browsing packages, building a custom menu, checking availability, and requesting a quote. This hero band occupies the top half of the content area, with ample whitespace around it. Beneath it, a service category grid flows across the width of the container, presenting key event types in a neat, multi-column layout with consistent gutters and breathing room. Below that, separated by another generous vertical gap, a featured package list presents individual catering packages in a similar grid-like pattern, visually aligned with the categories above. The overall effect is minimalist and structured: the eye moves smoothly from the header, to the hero intro and actions, then down through categories and featured packages without visual clutter, maintaining the sophisticated, slightly upscale tone of the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual, behind-the-scenes component that initializes at the very top of the page render tree, conceptually sitting above the header in the document flow. It does not occupy any visual space or alter the layout directly; instead, it ensures that all downstream visual components (intro summary, category grid, quick actions, featured packages) receive dynamic data before rendering. From a layout perspective, it behaves as if it is invisible and heightless, exerting no influence on spacing, alignment, or the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; executes data loading logic before or alongside initial render without occupying or reserving any space in the visual layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main navigation menu is embedded within a full-width header bar fixed to the top of the viewport. The header has a height roughly 72–96px (around 15–20% of the viewport height on desktop), with content centered horizontally inside a max-width container. Within this container, the navigation links are horizontally centered as a row of text items, each separated by 16–24px of horizontal space. Vertically, the links are aligned along a single baseline and have 8–12px of vertical padding above and below the text, giving a balanced, airy feel. The active page (Home) is indicated with a subtle underline or bottom border directly under the corresponding navigation item, echoing the mockup’s active state style. To maintain the editorial aesthetic, the typography is clean and the menu background remains a solid, muted color that contrasts gently with the white main content area beneath. There is clear whitespace (about 48–64px) below the header before the main content begins, reinforcing separation between global navigation and page content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed at the top of the viewport so it remains visible on scroll. The internal navigation row is centered within a max-width container and does not scroll horizontally; the bar’s height remains constant, and it casts a subtle shadow or uses a slight contrast shift to distinguish it from the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page intro summary occupies the left column of the primary hero content band, directly beneath the header and aligned to the main content’s max-width container. It starts approximately 64px below the bottom edge of the fixed header, creating generous top whitespace. Within a 12-column grid, this component spans about 4 columns (roughly one-third of the container width) positioned against the left margin. The headline sits at the top-left of this column, aligned on the same horizontal baseline as the top of the quick-action-panel on the right, echoing the mockup’s aligned heading and form start. Beneath the headline, one or two short paragraphs of descriptive copy extend downward, with a comfortable 16–24px vertical gap between headline and body text. Line lengths are intentionally kept moderate (45–70 characters) to preserve readability and the upscale editorial style. The right boundary of this text aligns with the internal grid, leaving open space between the intro text and the adjacent quick-action-panel, so the layout feels composed rather than crowded. The entire block is vertically centered relative to the height of the quick-action-panel, but its top still sits close to the hero’s start to emphasize the section title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, locked to the left 4 columns of the 12-column grid on wider screens. On smaller screens, it would naturally stack above the quick-action-panel, occupying full width, but within the context of the described desktop layout it remains a left-side column element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service category grid sits directly beneath the hero band (page-intro-summary + quick-action-panel) as the next full-width content section within the max-width container. There is a generous vertical gap—about 48–64px—between the bottom of the hero section and the top of this grid, clearly differentiating it as a new content band while keeping the overall flow continuous. The grid spans the full usable width of the container (all 12 columns) with equal left and right margins. Categories are arranged in a multi-column layout, typically 3 columns on desktop, each grid item occupying approximately 4 columns with consistent horizontal gutters of 24–32px and vertical spacing between rows of about 32–48px. Each category tile is a simple, card-like block with internal padding following the 16–24px scale, a clean title, and optional short supporting text, maintaining low visual noise. The top of the grid may include a subtle section heading aligned to the left grid edge, with the first row of categories starting 16–24px below it. The entire grid maintains the overall minimalist style, using whitespace between items to avoid clutter and keep the content scannable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the main content flow, expanding vertically as more categories are present while preserving the column and gutter structure. It remains centered within the max-width container and does not introduce horizontal scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-action-panel is the dominant interactive element in the hero band, occupying the right side of the main content area and visually echoing the form column from the mockup. It begins at the same vertical baseline as the page-intro-summary heading, approximately 64px below the header, and spans roughly 8 columns of the 12-column grid, anchored to the right edge of the content container. The panel is rendered as a tall, card-like block with a white or slightly off-white background, a subtle border or shadow, and internal padding of around 24–32px on all sides. Inside, key actions (e.g., Browse Packages, Build a Custom Menu, Check Availability, Request a Quote) are arranged in a vertical stack, each occupying nearly the full width of the panel. Each action row has generous vertical padding (about 12–16px top and bottom) and is separated from the next by 16–24px of space, mirroring the vertical rhythm of form fields in the mockup. If labels and subtext are used within each action, they follow a left-aligned, single-column layout for a clean scan pattern. The height of the panel exceeds the height of the text column on the left, reinforcing its prominence and drawing the eye down the list of actions. The right outer edge of the panel aligns with the overall content container’s right margin, creating a clean vertical line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content, attached to the right 8 columns of the grid in the hero section. As content grows, it extends downward but remains vertically aligned with the intro text’s top edge. It does not scroll independently; it moves with the page content beneath the fixed header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured package list is positioned immediately below the service-category-grid as another full-width section inside the same max-width container. A vertical gap of roughly 48–64px separates it from the categories above, continuing the spacious section rhythm. It uses a card grid layout similar to, but slightly more content-dense than, the service-category-grid. The section begins with a left-aligned heading at the top, with the grid of featured packages starting 16–24px beneath it. Packages are laid out in 3 columns on desktop, each card occupying around 4 columns with the same 24–32px horizontal gutters as the category grid and 32–48px vertical spacing between rows. Each card includes a package name, a brief description, and key metadata (e.g., starting price, guest count), but maintains generous internal padding (16–24px) to avoid feeling cramped. The bottom of this section has a bit of extra whitespace (32–48px) before any footer or subsequent content, allowing the page to end on a light, uncluttered note. The visual alignment of card edges and gutters is consistent with the category grid above, reinforcing a structured, editorial grid system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that grows vertically based on the number of featured packages. It remains fully within the 12-column centered container, respecting the same margins and not introducing horizontal scrolling. It visually anchors the lower portion of the homepage while preserving the airy feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-entry-point appears as a compact navigation link or icon-with-label positioned within the fixed top header, aligned to the far right of the max-width container. Horizontally, it sits on the same baseline as the center-aligned main-navigation-menu items but is separated from them by a clear horizontal gap (at least 32–48px), so it reads as a distinct, utility-oriented element. It may be styled with slightly higher contrast (e.g., a subtle outline or pill-shaped background) to stand out without breaking the minimalist tone. Vertically, it shares the same 8–12px padding above and below as the other navigation items, ensuring visual harmony. Its placement at the far right edge of the header leverages common patterns for cart or checkout access, making it easy to find without overcrowding the central navigation. The rightmost edge of the cart-entry-point aligns with the container’s right margin, creating a strong vertical boundary that also aligns with the right edge of the quick-action-panel below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the header, moving in tandem with the main-navigation-menu as the user scrolls. It remains anchored to the right side of the max-width header container and does not change position relative to the navigation links across different content states.

name: Packages Listing
title: Packages
description: Functional listing of event catering packages with category-based navigation, filters, and sorting. Users can refine packages by guest count, price per person, dietary options, and other attributes, then navigate to a specific package detail page.

core_features: Load and display top-level package categories in navigation using getPackageCategoriesForNavigation, with the active category synced to the categoryId URL parameter.

Fetch available filter and sort options for the active category using getPackageFilterOptions, and use them to build the filters UI (guest count presets, price range, course counts, dietary filters, etc.).

List packages for the active category with filters (guest count, price range, includesDessert, vegetarianOnly, courseCount, optional mealType/serviceStyle, optional glutenFreeFriendly) and sorting using listPackages.
Allow users to adjust guest count, price per person range, dessert inclusion, vegetarian-only, and course count filters, with changes reflected in URL parameters where defined.
Allow users to adjust additional in-page-only filters (such as mealType, serviceStyle, glutenFreeFriendly) that affect listPackages calls but are not stored in URL parameters.
Allow users to sort packages by price_low_to_high, price_high_to_low, rating, or name, with sortBy persisted in URL parameters.
Enable navigation to package_detail.html with the selected package ID and current guestCount filter via URL parameters when a package card is clicked.
Support sharing/bookmarking of filtered results by encoding the current filter state into URL parameters and rehydrating filter state on page load.
user_workflows:
Browse packages by category:
1. User arrives on packages.html, optionally with a categoryId URL parameter.
2. Page controller reads categoryId from URL; if absent or invalid it treats the category as a default category (e.g., all categories) based on business rules.
3. Page calls getPackageCategoriesForNavigation to retrieve all active categories and highlights the one matching categoryId.
4. Page calls getPackageFilterOptions using the effective categoryCode to obtain filter presets (guest counts, price range, course counts, available meal types/service styles, and supported dietary filters).
5. Page reads any other supported URL parameters (guestCount, pricePerPersonMin, pricePerPersonMax, includesDessert, vegetarianOnly, courseCount, sortBy) to build the initial filter state.
6. Page calls listPackages with the categoryCode and the derived filter/sort parameters.
7. User sees a list of packages, along with active category indication and filter controls.

Refine packages with filters:
1. User adjusts the guest count filter (e.g., selects a preset or enters a custom value) in the filters component.
2. Filters component emits an onFilterChange event with updated filter state to the page controller.
3. Page controller updates the guestCount URL parameter to reflect the new value and keeps other URL parameters intact.
4. Page controller calls listPackages again with the updated guestCount and current values for other filters.
5. Package list updates to reflect packages that match the new guest count.
6. The same flow applies when the user adjusts pricePerPersonMin/pricePerPersonMax, includesDessert, vegetarianOnly, or courseCount: URL parameters are updated and listPackages is re-called.
7. When the user adjusts additional filters like mealType, serviceStyle, or glutenFreeFriendly, these values are updated only in internal state (no URL parameter), and listPackages is re-called with these values included.

Sort packages:
1. User selects a sort option (price_low_to_high, price_high_to_low, rating, or name) in the sort control.
2. Sort control emits an onSortChange event with the selected sortBy value.
3. Page controller updates the sortBy URL parameter.
4. Page controller calls listPackages with the existing filters and the new sortBy.
5. Package list re-renders in the new order.

Change category:
1. User clicks on a category navigation item (e.g., corresponding to corporate_events, weddings, parties_birthdays, coffee_breaks, all_events).
2. Category navigation component emits an onCategoryChange event with the selected categoryCode.
3. Page controller updates the categoryId URL parameter to the new categoryCode and may reset or keep some filter URL parameters according to business rules (e.g., may reset guestCount or leave as-is).
4. Page controller calls getPackageFilterOptions with the new categoryCode to refresh available filter options.
5. Page controller reconstructs filter state from URL parameters (which now use the new categoryId) combined with new filter options.
6. Page controller calls listPackages with the new categoryCode and current filters.
7. Package list updates to show packages from the newly selected category.

Open a package detail:
1. User clicks on a package card or package name in the package list.
2. Package card component emits an onPackageClick event with the selected package.id and the current guestCount filter value.
3. Page navigates to package_detail.html by constructing a URL with id set to the selected package.id and guestCount set to the current guest filter value.
4. User is taken to the package detail page where further customization and actions occur.

Share or bookmark filtered results:
1. User refines filters and sorting until they see a set of packages they want to share or revisit.
2. Each filter/sort change has already been written into the URL parameters by the page controller.
3. User copies the browser URL or bookmarks the page.
4. When the URL is later opened, the page controller reads all relevant URL parameters (categoryId, guestCount, pricePerPersonMin, pricePerPersonMax, includesDessert, vegetarianOnly, courseCount, sortBy).
5. Page controller calls getPackageFilterOptions for the category from categoryId and rebuilds filter UI state accordingly.
6. Page controller calls listPackages with parameters derived from the URL and internal non-URL filters (if any default is applied), recreating the same view the original user saw.
interactions: Clicking a category navigation item updates the active category, writes the categoryId URL parameter, refreshes filter options via getPackageFilterOptions, and refreshes package results via listPackages.
Changing the guest count filter (via preset selection or direct input) updates the guestCount URL parameter and triggers listPackages with the new guestCount.
Adjusting the price range filter updates pricePerPersonMin and pricePerPersonMax URL parameters and triggers listPackages with the adjusted price range.
Toggling the includesDessert filter updates the includesDessert URL parameter and triggers listPackages with the updated includesDessert flag.
Toggling the vegetarian-only filter updates the vegetarianOnly URL parameter and triggers listPackages with the updated vegetarianOnly flag.
Changing the courseCount filter updates the courseCount URL parameter and triggers listPackages with the updated courseCount value.
Changing additional filters such as mealType, serviceStyle, or glutenFreeFriendly updates page-internal filter state (not URL) and triggers listPackages with these parameters included.
Selecting a sort option updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers listPackages to fetch packages in the requested order.
Clicking on a package card or its title navigates to package_detail.html with URL parameters id set to the selected package.id and guestCount set to the current guest filter value.
Clicking the header cart icon navigates to cart.html without altering current filter state.
Clicking the logo or breadcrumb link navigates to index.html without changing URL parameters on this page.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page parses the current URL's query parameters. The categoryId parameter determines the active category; if it is missing or invalid, the page falls back to a default category code according to business rules (e.g., a catch-all category). The page then calls getPackageCategoriesForNavigation to retrieve all categories and confirm the display and active state. Next, getPackageFilterOptions is called with the effective categoryCode. The response defines the available guestCountPresets, pricePerPersonRange, courseCounts, mealTypes, serviceStyles, and which dietary filters are supported. The page constructs an internal filter state object analogous to the PackageFilterState model, with fields for categoryFilter, guestCount, pricePerPersonMin, pricePerPersonMax, includesDessert, vegetarianOnly, courseCount, and sortBy. The initial values for guestCount, pricePerPersonMin, pricePerPersonMax, includesDessert, vegetarianOnly, courseCount, and sortBy are taken from URL parameters if present; otherwise, they are left undefined or set using neutral defaults (e.g., no filter applied) consistent with the filter options response. Additional filter fields such as mealType, serviceStyle, and glutenFreeFriendly are maintained only in this internal state and are not reflected in URL parameters, because they are not defined as incoming URL parameters. After state initialization, listPackages is called with categoryCode set from categoryId and filters mapped from the internal state: guestCount, pricePerPersonMin, pricePerPersonMax, mealType, serviceStyle, includesDessert, courseCount, vegetarianOnly, glutenFreeFriendly, and sortBy. When the user updates any URL-backed filter or sorting control, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (using browser history APIs or a full reload, depending on implementation) and then re-calls listPackages with the updated state. When only non-URL-backed filters (mealType, serviceStyle, glutenFreeFriendly) change, the page updates only the internal state and re-calls listPackages. This ensures that the URL always represents a shareable snapshot of the major filters and sorting, while additional refinements remain in-page only. Navigation to package_detail.html composes a URL including id (selectedPackageId from the Package model) and guestCount (from current filter state) so that the detail page can initialize based on the same guest count.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parameter parsing, calls to getPackageCategoriesForNavigation, getPackageFilterOptions, and listPackages, and manages the internal filter and sorting state. It passes data and callbacks to child components and ensures that the URL reflects the current filter/sort state.

Displays navigation items for each available package category returned by getPackageCategoriesForNavigation. Highlights the currently active category (matching categoryId) and allows the user to switch categories.

Provides controls for refining the package list based on guest count, price per person range, includesDessert, vegetarianOnly, courseCount, and any additional supported options from getPackageFilterOptions (e.g., mealType, serviceStyle, gluten-free flag). It initializes control ranges and options using getPackageFilterOptions and current URL-derived state and emits updated filter values to the page controller.

Displays available sort options provided by getPackageFilterOptions (sortOptions field) and allows the user to choose one. It updates the sortBy value in the internal PackageFilterState and triggers a refresh of the package list.

Shows a summary of the current results and filters, such as the number of packages returned by listPackages for the active filters and a concise representation of which major filters (guestCount, price range, dietary flags, courseCount) are active.

Renders the list of packages returned from listPackages as a collection of package cards. It receives an array of objects containing package (Package model), categoryName, displayPricePerPerson, and estimatedTotalForGuestCount, and passes each to a package-card component.

Represents a single package in the listing. Displays basic information from the Package model (name, description, mealType, serviceStyle, courseCount, dietary flags, rating) plus displayPricePerPerson and estimatedTotalForGuestCount. When clicked, it notifies the controller to navigate to the package_detail.html page.
Displays a message and optional guidance when listPackages returns an empty array for the current filters, and may offer a control to clear or relax filters (triggering a filters reset).
Displays a simple breadcrumb path including a link back to index.html. Clicking on the appropriate crumb navigates back to the homepage without modifying query parameters on this page.
Provides a clickable icon or link in the header that navigates directly to cart.html when clicked. This component does not modify filters or call any interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a constrained max-width content area centered in the viewport. The left text block occupies roughly 4 columns and the right form block around 8 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain a linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width fixed header at the very top containing centered category navigation and the cart link on the right; below that, the main content sits in a centered, max-width container on a white background. Inside the main content, breadcrumbs appear first, followed by a primary two-column section: a narrow left column (~4/12) for page-level information (page title, description) and a wider right column (~8/12) for all interactive package controls and results (filters at the top, then results summary, sort control, and the package list/empty state stacked vertically).

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain a linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width fixed header at the very top containing centered category navigation and the cart link on the right; below that, the main content sits in a centered, max-width container on a white background. Inside the main content, breadcrumbs appear first, followed by a primary two-column section: a narrow left column (~4/12) for page-level information (page title, description) and a wider right column (~8/12) for all interactive package controls and results (filters at the top, then results summary, sort control, and the package list/empty state stacked vertically).
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport is reserved for the fixed header bar with category navigation. Below the header, a generous 48–64px band of whitespace separates it from the main content container. Inside the container, the left information column occupies about one-third of the width (4 of 12 columns) and the right interactive column takes about two-thirds (8 of 12 columns). Vertical spacing between major subsections (filters vs summary/sort vs results grid) follows a 24–48px rhythm to keep the layout airy and structured.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps information density moderate to low. The left column content is minimal (title, short description, breadcrumbs) with short line lengths and plenty of buffer around it. The right column groups controls and results but uses full-width fields, generous internal padding (8–12px vertically per control, 12–16px horizontally), and consistent 16–24px gaps between form rows and blocks. Package cards in the grid have ample white space and clear separation, avoiding a cramped catalog feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first hits the colored header bar, reads the centered category navigation, and notices the cart link on the top-right. From there, the gaze drops into the main content area: first the breadcrumb trail at the top-left, then the page title and descriptive copy in the left column. From that anchor, attention is pulled to the larger right column, starting at the top of the filters form and then scanning downward through the filter options. Once filters are set, the eye moves slightly down to the results summary on the left of a horizontal row and the sort control on the right, then continues down into the vertical stack of package cards. If no results appear, the prominent empty state replaces the grid as the terminal point of this vertical reading path.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a modern, editorial-style catalog for catering packages. A full-width, fixed header with a muted background color spans the top, with package category links centered horizontally and a discreet cart link aligned to the right. Below, a calm expanse of white opens into a centered content container. At its top-left, a subtle breadcrumb trail leads into a clear 'Packages' heading and a short, well-spaced description occupying a narrow left column. To the right, a wider column begins at the same vertical baseline with a neatly framed filters form, its fields stacked in a steady vertical rhythm. Beneath the filters, a slim horizontal band balances the results summary on the left and the sort dropdown on the right, marking the transition into the listing area. The package grid then cascades down the right column, each card separated by generous gutters and surrounded by white space, while the left column remains mostly static and light, acting as contextual framing. When no packages match the filters, a centered empty state takes over the grid area, preserving the same spacious, minimal aesthetic. Throughout, the composition feels balanced and airy, with clear hierarchy and a strong top-down, left-to-right visual flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller manifests as the primary textual header block within the main content area. It sits inside the centered max-width container, directly beneath the breadcrumb navigation with about 16–24px of vertical separation. Horizontally, it occupies the left column of a 12-column grid, spanning approximately 4 columns (about one-third of the content width). The main page title ('Packages') appears at the top of this block, followed by a short descriptive paragraph in one or two compact lines, both aligned flush-left. There is generous padding above and below the text (24–32px total) so this block feels calm and uncluttered. Its top baseline aligns with the top of the filters form in the right column, reinforcing the paired two-column layout. It does not extend down the full height of the page; instead, it forms a visually anchored, self-contained text island that the rest of the page content flows around.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, anchored to the left column of the 12-column grid without any sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category navigation lives in the main header bar and serves as the central, horizontally oriented navigation element. It is centered within the full-width header, with its set of category links (e.g., Weddings, Corporate, Private Events) laid out in a single row. Each item has 8–12px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal spacing between neighboring items, maintaining the airy spacing system from the mockup. The active category is indicated by a slim, refined underline directly beneath the text label, spanning just slightly wider than the label itself. The nav row sits vertically centered within the header bar, with equal padding above and below, reinforcing a balanced, symmetrical feel. On wide screens, the nav appears roughly centered in the viewport, with adequate breathing room to the left and the cart link on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the full-width header, remaining visible at the top of the viewport as the user scrolls the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters form occupies the upper portion of the right column in the main content area, mirroring the position of a prominent form in the mockup. It starts on the same vertical baseline as the page title in the left column and spans approximately 8 of the 12 grid columns (about two-thirds of the content width). Visually, it appears as a single coherent panel with a white background (matching the page) but may be subtly framed with a fine border or a soft shadow to distinguish it as a control area. Inside, filter controls (guest count, price per person, dietary options, etc.) are stacked vertically, each taking the full internal width of this column. Each field has around 8–12px of vertical padding and 12–16px horizontal padding inside inputs, with 12–16px vertical gaps between fields to create a clear vertical rhythm. Filter section headings, if any, align left and are separated from their controls by 8px gaps. The bottom of the filter panel sits about 24–32px above the row containing the results summary and sort control, clearly separating 'filtering' from 'result viewing' while keeping them visually associated.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column on small screens; on larger screens it can be configured to become sticky to the top of the viewport once the user scrolls past its initial position, but it remains confined to the right column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is positioned directly beneath the filters form in the right column, as part of a slim horizontal status row. This row aligns flush-left and flush-right within the 8-column right section. On this row, the sort control sits on the far right, aligning its right edge with the right edge of the filters form and the package grid below. It typically includes a concise label ('Sort by') with a dropdown or button group. The control itself has 8–10px vertical padding and 12–16px horizontal padding, matching the input padding scale. There is about 24px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the filters form and this summary/sort row, and about 16–24px between this row and the top of the package list, making this row a clear pivot between filtering and browsing. On narrow screens, the sort control may wrap below the results summary but remains visually associated and aligned to the right side of the content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it scrolls away with the content and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary shares a horizontal row with the sort control and occupies the left side of this band directly below the filters form in the right column. Its left edge aligns with the left edge of the filters form, and it typically contains compact text such as '24 packages found' or a brief explanation of the current filter state. It is set in smaller, understated typography, contributing to the editorial feel. The vertical alignment of its baseline matches that of the sort control on the right, forming a tidy, balanced row. The summary does not dominate visually but remains immediately visible above the packages grid. There is consistent 16–24px vertical spacing below this row before the first row of package cards begins, reinforcing the separation between informational status and the listing content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package list occupies the bulk of the right column beneath the results summary and sort row. It spans the full width of the 8-column right section and is centered within the page’s overall grid by virtue of the main container. Package cards are arranged in a responsive grid: on large screens, two to three cards per row depending on available width, with even horizontal gutters of approximately 24px and vertical spacing between card rows of 24–32px. The top edge of the grid sits about 16–24px below the results summary/sort row, and the bottom of the grid has generous padding (48–64px) before the end of the main content area, maintaining the page’s spacious feel. The grid itself feels orderly and calm, not overly packed; each card has sufficient white space around it so that rows and columns are clearly perceptible without borders. On mobile, the list collapses to a single-column vertical stack, still maintaining 16–24px vertical gaps between cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; expands vertically as more packages are listed and scrolls under the fixed header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each package card is a self-contained vertical tile within the package list grid. It occupies one grid cell and maintains consistent width with its siblings in the same row. The card features a white background with subtle rounding on the corners and possibly a very light border or soft shadow to distinguish it from the page background while retaining a minimalist aesthetic. Internal padding is generous, around 16–24px on all sides. The content is stacked top-to-bottom: an optional thumbnail image or color block at the top spanning the card width, followed by the package name in bold type, the price per person and key meta details (guest count range, dietary tags) laid out with 8–12px vertical spacing, and finally a subtle call-to-action (e.g., 'View details') aligned either left or right at the bottom. Text lines are kept relatively short and succinct. There is about 12–16px vertical space between each textual cluster inside the card, giving a calm rhythm. The card’s height adapts to its content but stays visually balanced across the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the package-list grid; interacts on hover or focus but does not change its spatial placement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state appears in the same spatial footprint as the package list when no packages meet the current filters. It is centered within the right column, both horizontally and with generous vertical breathing room (at least 64px of padding above and below within the main content area). Instead of multiple cards, a single, clean panel or block displays a concise headline ('No packages match your filters'), a short explanatory sentence, and one or two suggested actions (e.g., 'Clear filters' or 'Browse all categories'). These elements are stacked vertically with 12–16px gaps, aligned center. The overall width of the empty state block is constrained to roughly 60–70% of the right column width so it feels focused and not sprawling. The surrounding white space maintains the same grid alignment as the cards would, ensuring that the empty state feels integrated, not like an overlay.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static replacement for the package-list area; it occupies the listing space only when there are zero results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits at the top of the main content container directly beneath the fixed header, separated from the header by a generous vertical gap of 48–64px to emphasize the transition into page content. It aligns with the left edge of the 12-column grid and spans only as wide as its text requires, keeping line length short. The breadcrumbs themselves are set in smaller, muted typography, with subtle separators between levels. There is about 8–12px of vertical spacing between the breadcrumbs and the page title block (packages-page-controller) below. This placement makes the breadcrumbs the first element the eye encounters when dropping below the header, but their subdued styling ensures they support rather than compete with the main title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content, scrolling normally beneath the fixed header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart link is positioned within the fixed header bar on the far right side, horizontally aligned with the centerline of the category navigation. It may appear as a minimalist icon with or without a small label ('Cart') and potentially a subtle badge for item count. There is 16–24px of horizontal padding between the cart link and the right edge of the viewport, providing a margin that echoes the container padding of the main content. Vertically, it is centered within the header’s height, maintaining a balanced look. The cart link is visually lighter than the navigation labels—perhaps a thinner icon stroke or lighter text color—so as not to overpower the central navigation, but its placement at the top-right corner makes it easy to find.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the header; it remains anchored in the top-right corner of the viewport as the user scrolls.

name: Package Detail
title: Package Detail
description: Configurable detail page for a single catering package where users set guest count, customize the menu, select up to two add-ons, optionally pick one or multiple event dates, and either add the configured package to the cart or start a quote request.

core_features: Load full package details using getPackageDetail based on the required id URL parameter and optional guestCount URL parameter for pricing preview.
Display package summary information (name, description, category, meal type, service style, rating, dietary friendliness).
Allow users to enter or adjust guest count and see pricing (per person and estimated total) update based on guest count and configuration.

Provide menu customization when allowsMenuCustomization is true, using menuSections from getPackageDetail and enforcing per-section selection constraints (minSelection, maxSelection, isRequired, allowOnlyVegetarian).
Present an add-ons selector when allowsAddOns is true, using availableAddOns from getPackageDetail, and enforce that at most two add-ons can be selected at once.

Allow event date selection when allowsDateSelection is true, including the ability to define multiple event dates that share the same guest count and configuration so that multiple dated line items can be created from the same package.

Add the currently configured package (or each configured date for multi-date scenarios) to the cart as CartItem entries via addPackageToCart, with guestCount, eventDate, selectedDishIds, selectedAddonIds, and an optional customDisplayName.
Initialize a quote request for the configured package via initQuoteRequestFromPackage and navigate to quote_request.html with URL parameters packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, and eventDate.
Provide navigation to cart.html (e.g., "View Cart") after at least one successful addPackageToCart call.
user_workflows: View and configure a single-date package then add to cart: 1) User opens package_detail.html with an id URL parameter (and optional guestCount). 2) Page reads id and guestCount from the URL and calls getPackageDetail(packageId=id, guestCountPreview=guestCount or undefined). 3) Package summary, default menu configuration, available add-ons, and initial pricing preview are displayed. 4) User adjusts guest count; the page updates price preview, optionally by re-calling getPackageDetail with the new guestCountPreview and/or recalculating locally using menu and add-on selections. 5) If allowsMenuCustomization is true, user selects dishes within each menu section respecting selection limits. 6) If allowsAddOns is true, user selects up to two add-ons. 7) If allowsDateSelection is true, user picks a single event date; otherwise, eventDate may remain unset or use any business rule for requirement. 8) User clicks "Add to Cart". 9) Page validates that required fields (guestCount, required menu sections, required date if applicable, and add-on rules) are satisfied. 10) Page calls addPackageToCart with packageId, guestCount, eventDate, selectedDishIds, selectedAddonIds, and optional customDisplayName. 11) On success, the page shows confirmation and enables navigation to cart.html (e.g., via a "View Cart" button).

Configure a package for multiple event dates (e.g., recurring coffee breaks) and add all dates to cart: 1) User opens package_detail.html with id and optional guestCount as in the single-date flow. 2) After getPackageDetail loads, the user sets guestCount, menu selections, and up to two add-ons. 3) Because allowsDateSelection is true, the user picks a first event date and adds it to an internal list of planned dates for this configuration. 4) User repeats date selection for additional dates, adding each to the list (the configuration of guestCount, menu, and add-ons remains shared across dates). 5) When the user is satisfied with the list of dates, they click an action such as "Add All Dates to Cart". 6) For each date in the internal list, the page calls addPackageToCart with the same packageId, guestCount, selectedDishIds, selectedAddonIds, and the specific eventDate. 7) If all calls succeed, the page clears the internal date list and exposes a success message and a "View Cart" navigation option. 8) If any call fails, the page reports which date(s) were not added so the user can retry or adjust.

Start a quote request from a configured package: 1) User opens package_detail.html with id and optional guestCount, which triggers getPackageDetail as in other flows. 2) User configures guest count, menu selections, up to two add-ons, and an event date when required. 3) User clicks a "Request Quote" action instead of adding to cart. 4) Page validates the same core requirements as for adding to cart (guestCount, required menu sections, optional date requirements). 5) Page calls initQuoteRequestFromPackage with packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, and eventDate. 6) On success, the page receives a response with the package, guestCount, selectedAddOns, and eventDate plus pricing estimates, and then redirects the browser to quote_request.html with URL parameters: packageId (from id), guestCount (current selection), selectedAddonIds (as a comma-separated list based on current selection), and eventDate (current selection). 7) User completes additional contact details and submits the quote request on quote_request.html.

Adjust configuration after initial load: 1) User lands on package_detail.html, which loads data via getPackageDetail. 2) User changes guestCount, menu selections, add-ons, and/or event date. 3) The page updates internal state and pricing preview; it may also update the URL guestCount parameter using history or location APIs so that a refreshed or shared link reflects the current guestCount. 4) When ready, user either adds to cart via addPackageToCart or initiates a quote via initQuoteRequestFromPackage with the current state. 5) If the user navigates away and later returns with the updated URL parameters, getPackageDetail is called again with guestCountPreview matching the URL guestCount value, restoring the previous guest count context.
interactions: On initial page load, the page reads the id and guestCount URL parameters; if id is missing, the page should present an error state instead of calling getPackageDetail. If id is present, the page calls getPackageDetail with packageId derived from id and guestCountPreview derived from guestCount if available.

When the user modifies the guest count input, the page validates that it is a positive number and respects any minGuests or maxGuests constraints from the Package data. On valid change, the page updates internal state, optionally updates the URL guestCount parameter, updates pricing preview locally, and may re-call getPackageDetail with the new guestCountPreview when dynamic pricing depends on guest count.

When the user selects or deselects dishes within a menu section, the page enforces that the number of selected items stays between minSelection and maxSelection when those values are defined, and ensures isRequired sections have at least one selected dish before allowing submission. The list of selected dish IDs is kept in internal state to be used in addPackageToCart.

When the user toggles add-on selections, the page restricts the total number of selected add-ons to at most two. If the user attempts to select more than two, the component prevents the additional selection or requires the user to deselect an existing choice first. The selected add-on IDs are stored in internal state. Certain actions (such as flows explicitly requiring two add-ons) can validate that the selectedAddonIds collection contains exactly two entries before proceeding.

When allowsDateSelection is true and the user interacts with the date picker, the page updates the current eventDate in internal state. For multi-date behavior, the user can add the current eventDate to a list of pending dates; the page validates that dates are not empty and avoids adding duplicate dates based on its comparison rules.

On clicking the primary "Add to Cart" action for a single date, the page validates guestCount, required menu sections, add-on selection rules, and (when relevant) eventDate. If validation passes, it calls addPackageToCart with packageId, guestCount, eventDate (or null/undefined when not used), selectedDishIds, selectedAddonIds, and an optional customDisplayName. For multi-date submissions, the page will call addPackageToCart once per stored date with the same configuration except for eventDate.

On successful addPackageToCart calls, the page may display a non-blocking confirmation state and expose a control for navigation to cart.html. The navigation uses a direct link without passing additional parameters (cart contents are determined by the existing server-side or session cart state).

On clicking the "Request Quote" control, the page validates configuration similarly to the add-to-cart flow. On success, it calls initQuoteRequestFromPackage with packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, and eventDate. After receiving a successful response, it navigates to quote_request.html with URL parameters packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds (comma-separated), and eventDate derived from the current page state.

If any interface call (getPackageDetail, addPackageToCart, initQuoteRequestFromPackage) returns an error or a non-success state, the page shows an appropriate error message and does not navigate away. In case of an error during a multi-date add-to-cart operation, the page informs the user which dates were successfully added and which were not, based on the sequence of addPackageToCart responses.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary external state is driven by URL parameters and the responses from getPackageDetail, addPackageToCart, and initQuoteRequestFromPackage. On load, the page reads the required id parameter; without id, it cannot call getPackageDetail and should instead show an error state. With id present, the page calls getPackageDetail(packageId=id, guestCountPreview=guestCountParamOrUndefined). If a guestCount URL parameter is present, the initial guest count field and price preview are set accordingly; otherwise, the page can derive an initial value from Package.minGuests when available or leave the field empty until the user provides a value. After load, user actions update internal, in-memory state only (guestCount, selectedDishIds by section, selectedAddonIds, eventDate or eventDate list, calculated per-person and total price). When guestCount changes, the page may update the guestCount URL parameter to keep the URL shareable, using non-destructive URL update mechanisms, without storing any configuration in localStorage. When the user clicks "Request Quote", the page uses the current in-memory state to call initQuoteRequestFromPackage and then constructs the redirect URL quote_request.html?packageId={id}&guestCount={currentGuestCount}&selectedAddonIds={csvOfSelectedAddonIds}&eventDate={currentEventDateISO}, ensuring that all necessary configuration is available on the next page through URL parameters only. When the user navigates to cart.html, no additional parameters are required because the cart contents have already been updated via addPackageToCart. Throughout, no static or hardcoded business data (such as fixed IDs, names, prices, or guest counts) is used; all such values come from URL parameters, user input, or API responses.

components.functionality: Controls initial data loading and high-level state for the package detail page. On page load, reads the id and optional guestCount URL parameters, calls getPackageDetail, distributes the resulting data to child components, and coordinates updates when guestCount changes and triggers new previews.
Displays non-editable package summary details such as name, category, description, mealType, serviceStyle, rating, and high-level dietary indicators to give context before configuration.

Provides a numeric input (or selector) for guestCount. Validates against Package.minGuests and Package.maxGuests when present and notifies the page controller about valid changes so that pricing previews and dependent components can update. May also update the guestCount URL parameter for shareable configuration.

Renders all menu sections from getPackageDetail.menuSections when allowsMenuCustomization is true. For each PackageMenuSection, lists associated PackageMenuItems and their Dish data, enforces minSelection and maxSelection, respects allowOnlyVegetarian by filtering to vegetarian dishes when true, and ensures required sections have at least one selected item. Collects selected dish IDs across all sections into a single selectedDishIds list for use by pricing and submission.

Shows available add-ons from getPackageDetail.availableAddOns when allowsAddOns is true. Lets users select or deselect add-ons while enforcing that at most two add-ons can be selected. Maintains a list of selectedAddonIds and emits changes to update pricing summaries and to be used by addPackageToCart and initQuoteRequestFromPackage.

Allows selection of an eventDate when allowsDateSelection is true or when business rules require a date. Supports choosing a single date for the current configuration and supplying it to addPackageToCart and initQuoteRequestFromPackage. Validates that eventDate satisfies required constraints when applicable.

When allowsDateSelection is true, manages an internal list of multiple event dates that share the same guestCount, menu selections, and add-ons. Provides controls to add the currently selected eventDate to a list, remove dates from the list, and trigger bulk add-to-cart, where addPackageToCart is called once per date with the common configuration.

Displays the current effective price per person and estimated total price based on getPackageDetail.effectivePricePerPerson, getPackageDetail.estimatedTotalPrice if guestCountPreview was provided, and any additional per-person amounts inferred from selected dishes and per-person add-ons, plus fixed-fee add-ons. Updates reactively when guestCount, menu selections, or add-on selections change.

Aggregates current configuration (guestCount from guest-count-form, eventDate from event-date-selector, selectedDishIds from menu-customization-sections, selectedAddonIds from addons-selector) and submits it via addPackageToCart as a single CartItem for the current eventDate. Handles validation before submission and shows success or error feedback. For multi-date flows, delegates to multi-date-builder for repeated addPackageToCart calls.

Uses the current page configuration to initiate a quote request. When clicked, validates configuration (guestCount, required menu sections, add-on constraints, eventDate requirements), calls initQuoteRequestFromPackage with packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, and eventDate, then navigates to quote_request.html with URL parameters packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds (comma-separated), and eventDate.

Provides navigation to cart.html and optional status messaging after successful addPackageToCart calls. Typically shows a "View Cart" or equivalent control only after at least one cart item has been successfully added during the current session.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a constrained max-width content area centered in the viewport. The left text block occupies roughly 4 columns and the right form block around 8 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s two-column composition beneath a full-width fixed header. The header (not part of these components) occupies the top band with centered navigation and an active state for the Package Detail section. Below it, the main content sits in a centered, max-width container using a 12-column grid: the left column (about 4/12) hosts the package summary, and the right column (about 8/12) contains a vertically stacked configuration form composed of guest count, menu customization, add-ons, event dates, pricing, and cart/quote actions. All components are arranged in a clear top-to-bottom flow, with each form section reading as a consecutive block within the right column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s two-column composition beneath a full-width fixed header. The header (not part of these components) occupies the top band with centered navigation and an active state for the Package Detail section. Below it, the main content sits in a centered, max-width container using a 12-column grid: the left column (about 4/12) hosts the package summary, and the right column (about 8/12) contains a vertically stacked configuration form composed of guest count, menu customization, add-ons, event dates, pricing, and cart/quote actions. All components are arranged in a clear top-to-bottom flow, with each form section reading as a consecutive block within the right column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly 15–20% of the viewport height is reserved for the full-width header. The main content begins with generous top spacing (around 64px) below the header, reinforcing a calm, editorial feel. Within the max-width content band, the left column takes about one-third of the width while the right column takes about two-thirds, emphasizing the configuration form. Inside the right column, each component spans nearly the full column width with consistent vertical gaps of about 24–32px between sections. Section padding and internal spacing follow the 8px base unit (e.g., 16–24px padding inside cards and panels) to maintain rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout uses a moderately spacious density: the left package summary is concise with short paragraphs and tight grouping of key info. The right column contains more elements but keeps them readable via clear grouping into sections, consistent vertical spacing, and full-width form controls. Inputs and selectors have comfortable padding (about 8–12px vertical, 12–16px horizontal) and enough separation (16–24px) to avoid a cramped appearance. No component feels overloaded; instead, the user experiences a linear, calm configuration flow with only one or two decisions per visual chunk.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first encounters the colored fixed header and centered navigation, locating the active Package Detail state. From there, attention drops to the left column where the package name and key details in the package summary anchor understanding of what is being configured. The eye then moves right to the start of the configuration stack, beginning at the guest count form and flowing down through menu customization, add-ons, and event date selection. Finally, attention settles on the price summary panel and the primary add-to-cart action at the lower right of the main content, with the request-quote option and cart navigation as secondary decision points. The vertical stacking and consistent spacing create a strong downward reading path that closely mirrors the form column in the mockup.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a clean, minimalist frame: a full-width fixed header with centered navigation spans the top, with the active Package Detail section subtly underlined. Beneath it, a generous band of whitespace leads into a centered content area constrained to a comfortable max width, set on a white background. This content band is split into two columns: on the left, a narrow column presents the package title, short description, and key details in a compact, editorial block; on the right, a wider column contains the entire configuration workflow as a single, vertically stacked form-like sequence. Starting at the same vertical baseline as the package title, the guest count form appears first, followed by menu customization controls, then add-ons, and finally event date selection and optional multi-date configuration. Below these, a price summary panel and action area (add to cart, request a quote, and cart navigation) close the flow and anchor the eye at the bottom right. The spacing is generous but disciplined, ensuring each section is clearly distinct yet visually connected, producing a calm, professional layout that mirrors the mockup’s two-column form-heavy composition.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-detail-loader behaves as a full-page overlay that temporarily sits above all other content. When active, it covers the entire viewport from the very top edge (over the fixed header) to the bottom edge, with a semi-opaque, slightly muted backdrop. At the center of the viewport, both horizontally and vertically, a simple, minimal loading indicator or message appears, with ample whitespace around it to maintain the clean, upscale aesthetic. When loading completes, this overlay fades out, revealing the underlying two-column layout without shifting any content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-screen fixed overlay that appears on top of all components while data is loading, then disappears completely.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-summary-header occupies the left column of the main content band, starting just below the header’s bottom edge with a vertical offset of about 64px. It spans roughly 4 of 12 columns, taking up about one-third of the overall content width. The top of this block aligns horizontally with the top of the guest-count-form in the right column, creating a shared visual baseline. Inside this area, the package title appears at the top in a prominent, dark type, followed by a short descriptive paragraph and a compact stack of key details (such as price per person, minimum guests, or category), all arranged with 8–16px spacing between lines and elements. The bottom of this column remains mostly open, leaving negative space to the left of the taller right-column form, enhancing the emphasis on the configuration process while keeping the summary easily accessible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored in the left column within the main content grid; it scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guest-count-form is the first and uppermost block in the right column, aligned so that its top edge sits on the same horizontal line as the package title in the left package-summary-header. It occupies roughly 8 of 12 grid columns (about two-thirds of the content width) and stretches from the right edge of the left column to the container’s right margin, with a comfortable gutter between columns. This block reads as a single, simple card or panel: a label and optional short helper text at the top, then a full-width numeric input or slider for guest count. Internal padding is around 16–24px on all sides, and the input itself uses around 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding. There is about 24–32px of vertical space below this form before the next configuration section begins, reinforcing its role as the entry point into the configuration flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the right column’s vertical stack, forming the top of the configuration flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The menu-customization-sections component sits directly below the guest-count-form within the right column, separated by a vertical gap of about 32px to clearly signal a new step. It spans the full width of the right column (around 8 of 12 grid columns), keeping a clean, flush alignment with the guest-count-form above. The component presents multiple menu groups or categories stacked vertically: each group can be treated as a sub-section with its own heading at the top, followed by one or more selection controls (checkboxes, radio options, or dropdowns) laid out in a single column or a restrained two-column grid, depending on content density. Each sub-section has consistent internal padding (16–24px) and 16–24px spacing between options, preserving the calm, editorial feel even with more options present. A subtle dividing line or extra vertical spacing (24–32px) separates each menu group, while the overall component reads visually as one continuous step in the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding section within the right column that grows taller as more menu options are included.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The addons-selector appears next in the right column, positioned below the menu-customization-sections with a vertical gap of about 32px. It continues the full-width alignment of the previous sections, occupying the same 8-column span. The section begins with a concise heading and optional short explanation line, followed by add-on choices. Each add-on can be presented as a card-like row with a title, brief description, and price, arranged in a vertical stack to maintain the strong downward reading path. For up to two selected add-ons, selection controls (checkboxes or toggles) are placed consistently at the left or right edge of each row. Internal spacing mirrors other sections: 16–24px padding within the block and 16px gaps between rows. The right edge of this component lines up with the right edge of the guest-count and menu sections, reinforcing alignment and order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the right column stack; its height adjusts based on how many add-ons are available, but it remains visually grouped as a single configuration step.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-date-selector is placed directly below the addons-selector, separated by around 32px of vertical whitespace. It retains the full width of the right column, maintaining the same left and right alignment as the sections above. At the top, a clear label or subheading indicates date selection, possibly with a brief description explaining that multiple dates can be added. Below, primary date input controls (such as a date picker or dropdown) are laid out in a single column, with each input spanning nearly the full width of the block. The component’s internal padding is consistent with others (around 16–24px), with 16–24px spacing between individual date controls.
