A subtle visual cue (for example, a link-style text or secondary button) near the bottom of this section invites users to add additional dates; when used, it visually leads into the multi-date-builder immediately below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form section in the right column, serving as the gateway into the multi-date-builder when multiple dates are required.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The multi-date-builder sits immediately beneath the event-date-selector, effectively extending that section when multiple dates are needed. It uses a slight indentation, border, or subtle background tint to appear as a nested block within the overall dates step, while still spanning nearly the full width of the right column. Each additional date configuration is arranged in a vertical list: rows with the date, per-date guest adjustments or notes, and a remove action, all stacked with 12–16px vertical spacing. Padding inside this area is comfortable (about 16–24px), and the block is separated from the base event-date-selector controls above by about 16–24px, keeping the relationship clear without crowding. If no extra dates are added, this area collapses to zero height and leaves the vertical rhythm intact between the event-date-selector and the subsequent pricing/actions sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered section that expands or collapses within the right column depending on whether multiple dates are configured; scrolls with the page like other form sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price-summary-panel anchors the lower portion of the right column and appears immediately below the event-date-selector and multi-date-builder stack, separated from the last date-related component by about 32px. It spans the full width of the right column, but feels slightly more compact in height than the large form sections above to convey a concise summary. Inside, line items such as base package cost, guest-related adjustments, add-ons, and total price are listed in a tidy vertical stack, with amounts right-aligned for easy scanning. The panel has more defined card styling than the form sections—subtle border or shadow and 16–24px internal padding—to visually distinguish it as a summary. There is modest spacing (16–24px) below this panel where the add-to-cart-form and request-quote-trigger sit, letting the user quickly relate the actions to the current pricing context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section positioned near the bottom of the configuration flow. Optionally, on taller pages it can become sticky within the right column once scrolled into view, maintaining a small offset from the fixed header (e.g., 80–96px), but it remains confined to the column’s width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart-form sits directly beneath the price-summary-panel, separated by around 24px, occupying the same full width of the right column. It is visually compact but highly prominent: a large primary button (e.g., "Add to Cart" or "Add Package to Cart") spans most of the column width, centered or left-aligned depending on the overall style. If there are minor controls (such as quantity confirmations or notes), they appear in a tight vertical stack above the button with 8–12px spacing. The button itself has comfortable padding (around 12–16px vertical and 24–32px horizontal), and there is 8–16px of spacing below it before the alternative request-quote-trigger is shown. This configuration creates a clear focal point at the bottom of the form, matching the emphasis seen in the mockup’s primary action area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static action block at the bottom of the right column’s main configuration stack, serving as the primary completion control for the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The request-quote-trigger is positioned immediately adjacent to or just below the add-to-cart-form within the right column. On wider screens, it appears as a secondary button aligned on the same horizontal line as the primary Add to Cart button, usually to its right, with a horizontal gap of about 16–24px. On narrower screens, it stacks below the primary button with a vertical spacing of around 8–16px, maintaining clear hierarchy. The button or link style is more understated—outlined or text-only—while still matching the overall typography and spacing system. Its width is generally smaller or equal to the Add to Cart button, and it stays visually anchored to the pricing and cart actions area, reinforcing it as an alternate path rather than a separate step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static secondary action tightly grouped with the add-to-cart-form; layout adapts between inline (desktop) and stacked (mobile) while staying within the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-navigation-controls component forms the final row of the right column’s content, located just below the action area with a vertical gap of about 24–32px from the request-quote-trigger. It stretches across the full width of the right column but uses a light, minimal treatment to keep it tertiary in the visual hierarchy. Typically, a subtle left-aligned link such as "Back to packages" appears on the left side, while a "View cart" or similar control sits aligned to the right, creating a balanced horizontal bar. The text size is slightly smaller or equal to body text, and padding above and below is modest (about 12–16px), preserving negative space beneath. This row visually and functionally closes out the configuration flow, guiding users either back to browsing or forward into their cart without competing with the main Add to Cart button.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, horizontally oriented control row at the bottom of the right column; scrolls out of view as the user moves upward, acting as the end of the page’s primary content.

name: Cart & Checkout
title: Cart & Checkout
description: Page for reviewing catering selections, adjusting guest counts, applying promo codes, and confirming order totals before proceeding.

core_features: Load and display the current cart using the getCartSummary interface, including all line items, per-person prices, line subtotals, and totals.
Represent each selected package or custom menu as a single line item with its guest count, event date (if available), and configuration snapshot.
Allow users to adjust guest counts per line item and update pricing in real time via updateCartItemGuestCount.
Allow users to remove individual line items from the cart using removeCartItem, updating totals accordingly.
Provide a promo code input where users can submit a code and apply it through applyPromoCodeToCart, updating discount and final totals.
Display an order summary panel reflecting subtotal, discounts, final total, and any applied promo code details from the Cart and PromoCode models.
Offer navigation to continue browsing packages (packages.html) and optionally check date availability (availability.html) with relevant URL parameters.
user_workflows:
View cart after adding items:
1. User navigates to cart.html (via header link or a "View cart" action from other pages).
2. On page load, the page-init logic calls getCartSummary with no parameters.
3. The response provides the Cart plus an array of items combining CartItem with either Package or CustomMenu metadata.
4. The cart item list renders one row per CartItem, showing snapshot name, item type (package/custom menu), event date (if present), guest count, per-person price, and line subtotal.
5. The order summary panel uses the Cart fields (subtotal, totalBeforeDiscount, totalAfterDiscount, promoDiscountAmount, currency) and any appliedPromoCode details to display totals.

Adjust guest counts for a line item:
1. In the cart item row, the user modifies the guest count field for a specific CartItem.
2. On change or on blur (depending on implementation), the component validates that the new guest count is a positive number.
3. If valid, the page calls updateCartItemGuestCount with parameters: cartItemId for that row, and the new guestCount value.
4. On success, the response includes an updated Cart and the updatedItem.
5. The UI updates that row’s guest count, perPersonPrice (if recalculated by backend), and lineSubtotal using updatedItem, and refreshes the order summary panel using the returned Cart.
6. If the call fails, an inline error message is shown for that row and the guest count reverts to the last known valid value.

Remove an item from the cart:
1. The user clicks a remove action on a specific cart item row.
2. The UI may optionally show a confirmation step; on confirmation, it calls removeCartItem with the cartItemId of that row.
3. On success, the returned Cart is used to refresh totals, and the removed item row is deleted from the cart item list.
4. If the cart has no remaining items, the page switches to the empty-cart state with a prompt to continue browsing packages.
5. On failure, an inline error message is shown and the item remains visible.

Apply or change a promo code:
1. In the promo code form, the user enters a promo code string into an input field.
2. The user submits the form, triggering applyPromoCodeToCart with the promoCode parameter equal to the user-entered string.
3. The API returns success status, an updated Cart, and appliedPromo (PromoCode) plus a message.
4. If success is true, the order summary panel rebinds to the updated Cart data, the applied promo code details are displayed (using appliedPromo), and any existing error messages are cleared.
5. If success is false, the UI shows the returned message as an inline error near the promo field while leaving existing totals unchanged.
6. If the URL contained a promo parameter on page load, the page-init logic pre-fills the promo input and may auto-submit applyPromoCodeToCart once the initial cart summary is loaded.

Continue browsing or check date availability:
1. From the cart, the user can click a "Continue browsing" action to add more items.
2. This navigates to packages.html with the URL parameter categoryId set to a value that indicates all events.
3. From the cart, if a "Check date availability" action is present, the user can click it to open availability.html.
4. The availability.html URL can optionally include query parameters derived from the current cart (for example, total guest count or the soonest eventDate), constructed on the client side without additional interfaces.
5. The cart remains unchanged; users can return to cart.html using standard navigation.
interactions: Page load: triggers getCartSummary to populate the cart item list and order summary, and reads URL parameters to determine if it should auto-apply a promo code or focus a particular item.

Editing guest count field: user input in a numeric field bound to each CartItem.guestCount; on change or blur, validates and calls updateCartItemGuestCount, then rebinding the updated CartItem and Cart totals.
Click remove item: user clicks a remove control for a CartItem; on confirmation, removeCartItem is called and the item list plus summary re-renders based on the returned Cart.
Promo code input change: user types into the promo code field, updating local component state but not calling an API until form submission.
Promo code form submit: calls applyPromoCodeToCart with the current promo input value; on response, updates Cart totals and applied promo display or shows an error message.
Click continue browsing: navigates to packages.html with a categoryId URL parameter set via client-side routing, without mutating the cart.

Click check date availability: navigates to availability.html with optional URL parameters derived from the cart (such as total guests or earliest event date), without calling additional APIs from this page.
Focus or highlight specific item: if a URL parameter indicates a CartItem to focus, the cart list scrolls to and highlights that item row on initial render, without additional API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Cart & Checkout page state is driven entirely by API responses from the assigned interfaces and URL parameters, without using localStorage. On initial load of cart.html, the page reads query parameters such as: (1) promo: if present, the promo code value is stored in temporary view state; after getCartSummary completes successfully, the page calls applyPromoCodeToCart with this promo value and rebinds the returned Cart and appliedPromoCode to the UI. (2) focusItemId: if present, after getCartSummary returns, the page searches the items array for a CartItem.id matching focusItemId; if found, that row is brought into view and optionally expanded or highlighted. This parameter does not modify data on the server. (3) ref or source: optional string identifier describing the origin page (for example, a package-detail page or custom menu builder); the cart uses this only to adapt navigation hints such as where a 'Back' link might point, without affecting cart contents. All pricing and discount values come from the Cart returned by getCartSummary or the update/apply interfaces. The promo field's visible value can be initialized from either the Cart.appliedPromoCode or the promo URL parameter, with the Cart value taking precedence once loaded. Any change to guestCount via updateCartItemGuestCount or item removal via removeCartItem causes the page to overwrite its local Cart and items state with the latest values returned by the API, ensuring consistency. URL parameters are not updated automatically when users change the cart; they are only read on load to influence view behavior (e.g., pre-filling promo, focusing an item, or setting navigation context).

components.functionality: Initializes the Cart & Checkout page, reads URL parameters, calls getCartSummary, and orchestrates initial state such as applying a promo code from the URL or focusing a specific cart item.

Displays all cart line items returned by getCartSummary, one row per CartItem, including name, item type (package or custom menu), event date (if any), guest count, per-person price, lineSubtotal, and any key configuration notes.

Represents a single cart line item, showing identifying information and providing controls to modify guest count and remove the item, while reflecting updated pricing when backend responses are received.
Numeric input control within each cart item row that allows the user to adjust the guest count for that item and propagates valid changes to the backend.

Handles the logic for updating a cart item’s guest count. When triggered by the guest-count-input component, it validates the new value and invokes updateCartItemGuestCount, then merges the returned updatedItem and Cart into the current view state.
Control in each cart item row that, when activated, removes the corresponding CartItem from the cart by calling removeCartItem and updates the UI based on the returned Cart.
Captures a promo code from the user, displays the currently applied promo code if one exists, and calls applyPromoCodeToCart on submission to update discounts and totals.
Shows monetary summary of the cart including subtotal, any promo discount, totalBeforeDiscount, totalAfterDiscount, and currency, and indicates whether a promo code is currently applied.
Displays when the cart has no items; hides the cart item list and summary, and emphasizes navigation to continue browsing packages.

Provides actions to leave the cart: (1) navigate to packages.html with a categoryId URL parameter configured for full package browsing; (2) optionally navigate to availability.html with URL parameters derived from the current cart such as total guest count or earliest event date.
Reads a focusItemId URL parameter on load and, after getCartSummary returns, identifies the matching CartItem row in the cart-item-list to scroll it into view and visually highlight or expand it.

Detects a promo parameter in the cart.html URL and, once the initial Cart has been loaded via getCartSummary, invokes applyPromoCodeToCart with that value to synchronize URL-driven promo codes with backend state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a constrained max-width content area centered in the viewport. The left text block occupies roughly 4 columns and the right form block around 8 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom structure with a full-width fixed (or consistently positioned) header bar at the very top, containing the site logo and centered navigation. Directly beneath the header, a spacious white main content band is centered within the viewport, constrained to a max-width and aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. Within this band, the layout is split into two columns: the left column (about 4 of 12 columns) holds the cart page title, a brief descriptive line, and the vertically stacked cart-item-list; the right column (about 8 of 12 columns) holds a vertically stacked block composed of the order-summary-panel, promo-code-form, and navigation-actions, treated visually as a primary action area similar to a form. Behavioral components act on these visible structures without introducing separate visual blocks.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom structure with a full-width fixed (or consistently positioned) header bar at the very top, containing the site logo and centered navigation. Directly beneath the header, a spacious white main content band is centered within the viewport, constrained to a max-width and aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. Within this band, the layout is split into two columns: the left column (about 4 of 12 columns) holds the cart page title, a brief descriptive line, and the vertically stacked cart-item-list; the right column (about 8 of 12 columns) holds a vertically stacked block composed of the order-summary-panel, promo-code-form, and navigation-actions, treated visually as a primary action area similar to a form. Behavioral components act on these visible structures without introducing separate visual blocks.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height is dedicated to the full-width header. Below that, there is a generous 48–64px vertical gap before the main content band begins. The main content occupies a centered strip spanning about 70–80% of the viewport width (max-width around 1120–1200px), with left and right margins providing breathing room. Inside this strip, the left column occupies roughly one-third of the width, and the right column roughly two-thirds, echoing the 4/8 column split. Vertical spacing between major content groups (page title, cart list, summary area, actions) stays in the 32–64px range, while inner spacing between individual items or fields uses the 8–24px scale to maintain a calm but readable density.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderately low. The cart-item-rows are vertically stacked with consistent spacing, avoiding a cramped table look; each row has comfortable padding and clear separation. The order-summary-panel and promo-code-form echo form-like spacing: fields and lines of information are separated by 12–24px, with sufficient padding inside the panel (24–32px) to maintain a refined editorial feel. Text blocks, such as the page title and supporting copy above the cart, use short paragraphs and limited line lengths, reinforcing clarity and reducing visual noise.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first encounters the colored header bar and centered navigation, where the 'Cart' or 'Checkout' nav item is subtly highlighted. From there, the visual flow drops into the main content, landing first on the left-aligned page heading at the top of the left column. The brief descriptive text below it is read next, leading the eye down into the vertical rhythm of the cart-item-list. In parallel, the tall, consolidated right-hand block (order-summary-panel with promo-code-form and navigation-actions) exerts a strong pull, similar to the form column in the mockup. After scanning the cart items, the user’s attention naturally shifts to the right summary column to confirm totals, apply a promo, and finally to the primary checkout action at the bottom of that column, creating a clear left-to-right, top-to-bottom completion path.

layout.overall_description: The Cart & Checkout page sits beneath a full-width header bar with centered navigation, aligned to a minimalist, editorial aesthetic. Below the header, a generous band of white space introduces a centered main content area, constrained to a max-width and aligned to a 12-column grid. This area is split into two columns: on the left, a narrower column begins with a clear, dark page title like 'Your Catering Order', followed by a single short line of explanatory copy. Directly below, a vertically stacked list of cart-item-rows runs downward, each row occupying the full width of the left column with crisp separation and comfortable padding. On the right, a wider column begins on the same vertical baseline as the left title, containing a single, tall, card-like order-summary-panel. Within this panel, line items for subtotal, service fees, taxes, and total are arranged with a strong vertical rhythm. Just above or within the upper section of this panel sits the promo-code-form, styled as a compact form field plus button, aligned with the broader form-like aesthetic. At the bottom of the right column, aligned to the panel, the navigation-actions area presents the secondary link-style action and the primary checkout button, with the primary action visually emphasized. Behavioral components quietly enhance this experience by updating totals when guest counts change, highlighting focused cart rows, and auto-applying promo codes, all without disturbing the clean and spacious structure. When the cart is empty, the two-column structure collapses into a single centered message block that inherits the same refined spacing and typography, keeping the overall look cohesive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component establishes the overall frame of the Cart & Checkout page. It positions a full-width header bar fixed or consistently anchored at the top of the viewport, occupying roughly 15–20% of the viewport height. This header contains centered horizontal navigation and sits on a muted colored background band that spans edge-to-edge. Below the header, cart-page-init creates a generous vertical gap of about 56px (in the 48–64px range) before introducing the main content band. This main band is centered horizontally with a max-width of approximately 1120–1200px and side margins that scale with viewport width. Inside, it sets up an implicit 12-column grid: the left column covers approximately 4 columns (~33% of the content width), and the right column covers about 8 columns (~67%). All subsequent components—cart-item-list, order-summary-panel, promo-code-form, navigation-actions, and empty-cart-state—are positioned within this grid framework, ensuring that typography, spacing, and vertical rhythm follow the 8px-based spacing scale.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static layout infrastructure; defines fixed header region and a centered, non-sticky main content grid that scrolls as a whole beneath the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-item-list resides in the left column of the main content band. It begins just below the page heading and its brief introductory text so that the top edge of the first cart-item-row aligns closely with the top of the order-summary-panel on the right, preserving a shared baseline. The list spans the full width of the left column (about 33% of the overall content width) and extends vertically down as many rows as needed, maintaining consistent 16–24px vertical spacing between rows. A subtle top padding of around 16px separates the list from the heading above, and a bottom padding of around 32px provides breathing room before any potential footer or page end. The list background remains the same white as the main content area, relying on spacing and low-contrast dividers or alternating row backgrounds to visually delineate items without adding visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack within the left column; scrolls naturally with the page while maintaining alignment to the right column’s top baseline.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart-item-row is a horizontally oriented block filling the full width of the cart-item-list’s left column. The row has internal horizontal padding of about 16–24px and vertical padding of 12–16px to create a comfortable interaction target and maintain a refined appearance. Content within the row is arranged from left to right: the dish name and brief description align to the far left, using 1–2 short lines of text; to the right of this, separated by 16–24px, sits compact pricing and per-person details; the guest-count-input is positioned toward the right-middle of the row; and the line total is aligned to the far right, vertically centered with the input. A small remove-item-button is placed near the item name—either as a subtle text link or icon below the name with about 8px vertical spacing, or aligned to the far right under the total—ensuring that it does not dominate the row visually. Rows are separated by 8–16px of vertical gap or a light divider line, preserving the overall vertical rhythm. When focused or hovered (via the focused-item-highlighter), the row may show a subtle background tint or a fine border, but its size and spacing remain unchanged.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static row elements that expand the list vertically; they maintain their allocated width within the left column and adjust height based on content while preserving consistent padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guest-count-input is embedded within each cart-item-row, positioned toward the right side between the item details and the line total. It appears as a compact numeric input or stepper-style control, with a width sufficient for 2–3 digits (e.g., 56–72px) to maintain a tidy look. Vertically, it is centered within the row height, with internal padding of about 8–10px vertically and 12–16px horizontally, matching the form-control sizing used in the mockup’s form. The control aligns to an invisible vertical grid line so that all guest-count-inputs in successive rows line up neatly, reinforcing the columnar structure. There is a small horizontal gap (8–16px) between the input and the adjacent price/total text, ensuring clear separation without breaking the compact, form-like appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the cart-item-row; its width is fixed and does not reflow other text excessively, preserving the row’s horizontal layout on desktop. On narrow screens, it can shift to its own line beneath item details while maintaining consistent vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guest-count-update-handler has no direct visual footprint but influences the layout behavior of cart-item-row and order-summary-panel. When a guest-count-input changes, this handler updates the line total in the same cart-item-row and the aggregated values in the order-summary-panel, all within the existing layout, without triggering additional panels or overlays. The recalculated numbers replace the existing values in place, preserving the vertical rhythm and keeping the right column’s card dimensions stable except for minimal text width changes. Any subtle feedback (such as a brief highlight of updated totals) is contained within the current bounds of the row and panel, avoiding jumps or shifts in surrounding content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavioral component; works behind the scenes to update inline text and numeric values within existing layout slots without altering spatial structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-item-button is rendered inside each cart-item-row as a small, low-emphasis control. Typically it appears as a muted text link (e.g., 'Remove') or an understated icon placed directly below the item name with about 8px vertical spacing, aligned to the left edge of the row’s text block. Alternatively, it can be aligned to the far right beneath the line total, provided it remains visually secondary. Its hit area is comfortably padded (at least 8px vertically and 12px horizontally) but the visual styling remains light to avoid competing with pricing and guest counts. The button does not change row height significantly; it fits within the row’s existing padding and vertical rhythm. When an item is removed, the entire cart-item-row smoothly collapses, and the vertical gap between surrounding rows closes while preserving the list’s overall spacing and alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline control within cart-item-row; interacts by removing its host row and allowing the list to reflow vertically, with no lateral layout changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promo-code-form occupies a dedicated block within the right column, integrated with the order-summary area. On desktop, it sits either at the top of the order-summary-panel (directly under the panel’s title) or immediately above the panel as a visually connected bar, but still within the same 8-column right grid. The form spans nearly the full width of the right column, with inner padding of about 16–24px if it’s within the panel, or similar spacing around if it’s a separate but adjacent strip. The promo input field follows the form-control sizing from the mockup: about 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding inside the input, with a width of roughly 70–80% of the panel’s inner width. The 'Apply' button sits to the right of the field, aligned on the same baseline and separated by 8–16px, or stacks below on narrow screens. Vertical spacing above and below the promo-code-form is about 16–24px, aligning it with the rest of the panel’s vertical rhythm. The form’s styling is understated and cohesive with the order-summary-panel, so it feels like part of a unified action block rather than a separate section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form block within or immediately attached to the order-summary-panel; it does not scroll independently, and its elements reflow from horizontal to vertical stacking on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-summary-panel is the dominant block in the right column, echoing the visual role of the form column in the original mockup. It begins on the same vertical line as the page title in the left column, occupying the full width of the right column (~67% of the main content width) and presenting as a card-like panel on a white background with a subtle border or soft shadow. The panel has generous internal padding of 24–32px on all sides, and its content is arranged in a strict vertical stack: at the top, a concise heading such as 'Order Summary'; beneath that, the promo-code-form (if integrated inside); below, a series of summary lines (Subtotal, Service Fee, Taxes, etc.) each on their own row, separated by 8–16px; and near the bottom, the grand total displayed with slightly larger, darker typography and more spacing above (16–24px) to emphasize it. At the very bottom of the panel or immediately below it, the navigation-actions are aligned to the right edge. The panel extends vertically according to content but maintains a clean column of information with consistent line lengths and spacing, slightly taller and visually more prominent than the left column’s content band, reinforcing its role as the focal action area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky card within the right column; scrolls with the page as a whole. On tall carts, it may appear shorter than the total height of the cart list, but its top-aligned position remains consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-cart-state replaces the two-column cart content when there are no items in the cart. Instead of rendering cart-item-list and order-summary-panel, the main content band displays a single, centered message block within the same max-width container. This block is horizontally centered and constrained to about 4–6 columns (roughly 40–50% of the content width) to keep the message compact and readable. It sits below the header with the same 48–64px top margin, and vertically there is ample whitespace above and below, giving it a calm, airy feel. The block may contain a simple heading like 'Your cart is empty' with a short line of explanatory text beneath, and a subtle navigation action (e.g., a button or link to 'Browse Menus') centered below with 16–24px spacing between elements. The overall empty state respects the same minimalist, editorial aesthetic, using whitespace and typography rather than heavy graphic elements to convey the state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional central content block that appears only when no cart items exist; it is statically centered within the main content area and scrolls with the rest of the page if content height exceeds the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-actions component anchors the primary and secondary actions associated with the cart and checkout. On desktop, it is visually tied to the order-summary-panel in the right column. Typically, the actions appear at the bottom of the summary area: the primary action (e.g., 'Proceed to Checkout') is presented as a full-width or right-aligned button spanning about 50–70% of the panel’s inner width, with a height of roughly 40–48px and generous horizontal padding (16–24px). A secondary action (e.g., 'Continue Browsing' or 'Back to Menu') is placed to the left of or below the primary button, styled as a low-emphasis text link or ghost button. Vertical spacing between the last summary line and these actions is about 24px, creating a clear separation and visual hierarchy. The entire navigation-actions block aligns to the right column’s grid and sits within or directly under the panel, ensuring the user’s eye naturally falls from the summary total to the checkout button. On smaller screens, the actions stack vertically, with the primary button full-width and the secondary action below it, maintaining ample vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static action block associated with the order-summary-panel; it stays at the bottom of the summary area and scrolls naturally with the page, with responsive reflow from horizontal alignment to vertical stacking on narrow devices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The focused-item-highlighter is a behavioral component that subtly modifies the appearance of a cart-item-row when the user interacts with it (e.g., hovers over the row, focuses the guest-count-input, or taps it on touch devices). It does not change the layout’s dimensions but overlays a gentle visual treatment such as a soft background tint, a 1px border, or a thin left-accent line within the existing bounds of the row. This effect is confined to the row’s padded area so that surrounding spacing, row height, and the overall vertical rhythm remain unchanged. The highlight makes the active row stand out against the otherwise calm, white background of the cart-item-list, enhancing scannability and interaction clarity while adhering to the minimalist, upscale aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-structural overlay behavior within the confines of each cart-item-row; does not alter spacing, padding, or alignment, only the visual styling of the existing row area on interaction.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promo-from-url-handler is an invisible behavioral layer that interacts with the promo-code-form and order-summary-panel. When a promo code is present in the URL, this handler pre-populates the promo input field within the right column’s promo-code-form and triggers the 'apply' logic, updating the summary values in place. Any confirmation or error message generated appears within the existing promo-code-form area under the input, using a compact text line that fits within the current vertical spacing (e.g., 8–12px below the field), so the panel height adjusts only slightly. No new panels or overlays are introduced. The handler maintains the page’s spatial structure, ensuring that all changes remain contained within the right column’s card and do not disturb the alignment or proportions of the two-column layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely behavioral; it operates within existing promo-code-form and order-summary-panel boundaries, causing only text and numeric content updates without altering overall layout or component positions.

name: Availability & Booking
title: Availability & Booking
description: Check catering availability by date and time, select an available slot, enter guest count, and start a booking that continues on the Booking Details page.

core_features: Load availability data for a specific calendar month and year using getAvailabilityCalendar, based on URL parameters.
Display a navigable month calendar that indicates on which dates there are available slots, including specific marking for dates that have evening-period availability.
Allow users to navigate to previous and next months; navigation updates URL month/year parameters and reloads availability data via getAvailabilityCalendar.
Allow users to select a calendar day and see all available time slots for that day (including evening slots identified via the period field of AvailabilitySlot).
Allow users to select a specific availability slot (date and time) from the list of that day’s slots, restricted to slots where isAvailable is true.
Provide a guest count input associated with the selected availability slot, with validation against the slot’s maxGuests when provided.
Provide an optional control to filter or highlight evening-period slots to support tasks such as finding the earliest available evening on a Saturday in a given month.
Create a draft Booking via initBookingFromAvailabilitySlot using the selected availabilitySlotId and guestCount when the user chooses to continue.
On successful booking creation, navigate to booking_details.html with the bookingId returned from initBookingFromAvailabilitySlot as a URL parameter.
Handle loading and error states for availability retrieval and booking initialization (e.g., retry options if getAvailabilityCalendar or initBookingFromAvailabilitySlot fails).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Direct visit and basic booking
1. User opens availability.html without month/year parameters.
2. The page reads URL parameters (none found), derives a default month and year from the current date, and calls getAvailabilityCalendar with these values.
3. Once slots are loaded, the calendar displays the month with indicators for days that have any isAvailable slots; days with at least one evening-period slot are visually distinguishable.
4. User clicks on a date; the page filters the loaded slots to those whose date matches the selected day and shows them in a time slot list.
5. User selects one of the available time slots from the list (only slots with isAvailable = true are selectable).
6. User enters a guest count into the guest count input; the page validates that it is a positive number and, if the selected slot has maxGuests, that the guest count does not exceed maxGuests.
7. User clicks a Continue/Next button.
8. The page calls initBookingFromAvailabilitySlot with the selected availabilitySlotId and the entered guestCount.
9. On success, the page reads booking.id from the response and navigates to booking_details.html?bookingId=[booking.id].
10. If the call fails, an error message is shown and the user can retry the Continue/Next action.

Workflow 2: Pre-positioned month/year from another page
1. User clicks a link such as “Check availability” elsewhere on the site that points to availability.html with month and year query parameters.
2. On load, the page reads month and year from the URL and calls getAvailabilityCalendar with those exact values (no additional filtering or adjustments).
3. The calendar opens directly to that month/year with appropriate availability indicators, using the returned AvailabilitySlot data.
4. User optionally navigates to adjacent months using previous/next controls; each navigation action updates the URL query string with the new month and year and triggers a new getAvailabilityCalendar call.
5. User then continues as in Workflow 1: selects a date, selects a slot, enters guest count, and clicks Continue to initiate a Booking via initBookingFromAvailabilitySlot.

Workflow 3: Finding an evening slot that meets constraints (e.g., earliest available evening in a visible month)
1. User opens availability.html (with or without pre-set month/year) and the page loads availability for that month via getAvailabilityCalendar.
2. User enables an evening filter or uses a control that highlights only evening-period slots (period = "evening") within the currently loaded month.
3. The calendar highlights days that have at least one available evening-period slot; optionally, the list of slots for a selected day can be filtered to evening slots only.
4. If the user wants, they may also filter by day-of-week (implemented purely client-side using the date field of AvailabilitySlot, without additional API calls), to support selection such as only Saturdays within the loaded month.
5. The system scans the already loaded AvailabilitySlot data (client-side) to determine the earliest day in the current month and the earliest slot on that day that matches the user’s criteria (e.g., evening period and a given day-of-week). The result is used to pre-select that slot or scroll/focus to it.
6. User confirms this automatically suggested slot (or picks a different one), enters a guest count that complies with maxGuests if present, and clicks Continue.
7. The page calls initBookingFromAvailabilitySlot with that slot’s id and the guest count, then navigates to booking_details.html?bookingId=[booking.id] on success.
interactions:
Page load / initialization:
- On initial load, parse URL query parameters month and year.
- If both parameters are valid, use them directly; otherwise, compute a default month/year from the current date.
- Trigger a call to getAvailabilityCalendar(month, year).
- While loading, disable date and slot selection interactions and indicate loading state for the calendar.
- On success, populate internal state with the returned slots and render the calendar grid and any associated derived data (e.g., which days have evening slots).
- On error, show a retry control that, when clicked, re-invokes getAvailabilityCalendar with the same month and year.

Month navigation:
- When user clicks a “Next month” control, increment the current month/year in internal state, update the URL query string (?month=[newMonth]&year=[newYear]) using history/state APIs without a full page reload (or via full reload if necessary), and call getAvailabilityCalendar with the new values.
- When user clicks a “Previous month” control, decrement the month/year accordingly, apply the same URL update, and calls getAvailabilityCalendar again.
- While the new request is in flight, show a loading state and temporarily disable further navigation or selection to prevent conflicting actions.

Day selection:
- When the user clicks on a specific calendar day cell, if there are any AvailabilitySlot records whose date falls on that day, mark that day as the selected date in state.
- Filter the loaded slots to those matching the selected date and update the day-slot list component with that subset.
- Clear any previously selected slot if it is not part of the newly selected date’s slots.

Slot selection:
- In the day-slot list, each slot entry is derived from an AvailabilitySlot whose date matches the selected date and isAvailable is true.
- If an evening-filter toggle is enabled, slots where period is not "evening" can be hidden or visually de-emphasized (handled client-side based on the slot’s period value).
- When the user clicks/selects a slot, store its id as the currently selected availabilitySlotId in page state and update any summary component to display the slot’s date and startTime/endTime.
- If a user attempts to select a slot where isAvailable is false (e.g., via keyboard focus or older state), ignore the action and show a message if needed.

Guest count input:
- User enters a guest count into a numeric input field associated with the currently selected slot.
- On change or blur, validate that the input is present, numeric, and greater than zero.
- If the selected slot has maxGuests set, validate that guestCount is less than or equal to maxGuests; if it exceeds, display a validation error and prevent continuation until corrected.
- Store the valid guestCount in page state so it can be used when calling initBookingFromAvailabilitySlot and for display in the summary.

Evening filter / highlighting:
- User can toggle an option that filters or emphasizes evening-period availability.
- When enabled, the calendar component uses the existing slots data to mark days that have any slot with period = "evening" and isAvailable = true.
- The day-slot list, when a date is selected, filters to showing only slots with period = "evening" (or provides an option to show all versus only evening).
- This interaction triggers no new API calls; it operates purely on the slots already retrieved via getAvailabilityCalendar.

Continue / Next (booking initialization):
- When user clicks Continue/Next, check that:
  - A slot is selected (availabilitySlotId is set).
  - A guestCount is present and passes all validations.
- If validations fail, display errors and do not call the API.
- If validations pass, disable the Continue/Next button and call initBookingFromAvailabilitySlot with parameters availabilitySlotId and guestCount.
- On API success, read booking.id from the response booking object and redirect to booking_details.html?bookingId=[booking.id].
- On API error, re-enable the Continue/Next button, present an error state, and allow user to retry the action without losing their selections.

Reload / direct URL editing:
- If the user reloads the page or manually edits the URL query parameters for month and year, the initialization logic re-runs, reads the new parameters, and calls getAvailabilityCalendar accordingly.
- Any previously selected date/slot/guestCount from before the reload is not preserved (no localStorage or server persistence is used for these); the user begins fresh based on the new month/year state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary persistent state for this page is derived from URL query parameters and API responses. On page load, the script reads month and year from the URL (e.g., availability.html?month=[1–12]&year=[4-digit]). If both parameters are valid numbers, they are used directly in a call to getAvailabilityCalendar. If either is missing or invalid, the page computes a default month/year from the current date at runtime (without hardcoded values) and uses those for getAvailabilityCalendar. The response populates an in-memory state object containing the current month, current year, and the list of AvailabilitySlot records for that month. Derived view state—such as which days have any isAvailable slots, which have evening-period slots, which day is currently selected, which availabilitySlotId is selected, whether the evening filter is on, and the entered guestCount—is maintained in memory only for the current page instance and is not synchronized back to the URL, except for the month/year. When the user clicks previous or next month, the page updates both its internal month/year state and the URL query string (?month=[newMonth]&year=[newYear]), then calls getAvailabilityCalendar again using the updated parameters. This ensures that if the user shares the URL or refreshes the page, the same calendar month/year is loaded. When the user successfully initiates a booking via initBookingFromAvailabilitySlot, the page reads booking.id from the returned Booking object and navigates to booking_details.html?bookingId=[booking.id]. No other booking-related details are encoded in the URL from this page; the bookingId is the sole parameter forwarded to booking_details.html. The page does not use localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms; all state is derived from URL parameters, live API calls, and in-memory variables for the duration of the page session.

components.functionality: Reads and manages the calendar positioning state (month and year) based on the URL query parameters, triggers getAvailabilityCalendar when month/year changes, and updates the URL when the user navigates between months.
Encapsulates calls to getAvailabilityCalendar using the current month and year from the state manager, tracks loading and error status, and exposes the resulting slots to other components.

Renders a calendar grid for the current month and year using the slots loaded by availability-calendar-loader. Marks days that have any available slots, with a distinct indicator for days that have at least one slot where period is 'evening'. Allows the user to change the selected day and to navigate to previous/next months via callbacks to the state manager.

Provides a toggle or filter control that, when enabled, causes the calendar and day-slot list to focus on or filter to evening-period slots only (period = 'evening'), implemented entirely by filtering the currently loaded AvailabilitySlot data client-side.

Displays a list of AvailabilitySlot entries for the currently selected date. Only slots whose date matches the selected day and where isAvailable is true are shown as selectable items. The list respects the evening-filter-toggle state (e.g., can show only evening-period slots). The user can select a single slot from this list.

Captures and validates the guestCount for the currently selected availability slot. Ensures guest count is present, numeric, and greater than zero, and if the selected AvailabilitySlot has maxGuests defined, ensures guestCount does not exceed that value. Exposes the validated guestCount to the booking initiation component.

Shows a read-only summary of the user’s current choices: selected date, selected time slot (startTime/endTime and period), and entered guestCount. Updates automatically when the selected slot or guestCount changes, and disables its display when either is not yet selected.

Coordinates the final step of starting a booking. When the user clicks Continue/Next, it verifies that a slot is selected and that guestCount is valid. If validations pass, it calls initBookingFromAvailabilitySlot with availabilitySlotId and guestCount. On success, it obtains booking.id from the returned Booking object and redirects the browser to booking_details.html?bookingId=[booking.id]. On error, it surfaces an error state and allows retry.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a constrained max-width content area centered in the viewport. The left text block occupies roughly 4 columns and the right form block around 8 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the mockup’s structure: a full-width fixed header with centered navigation at the top, followed by a single main content band on a white background. Inside this band, use a two-column layout on a 12-column grid: the left column (≈4/12 width) holds the page title and contextual copy for Availability & Booking plus a concise selected-slot summary; the right column (≈8/12 width) acts as the primary interaction area, stacking the availability calendar, time-slot list, guest count form, and booking initiation control in a clear top-to-bottom flow. Invisible logic components (state manager and loader) are visually tied to, but not separate from, the calendar area.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the mockup’s structure: a full-width fixed header with centered navigation at the top, followed by a single main content band on a white background. Inside this band, use a two-column layout on a 12-column grid: the left column (≈4/12 width) holds the page title and contextual copy for Availability & Booking plus a concise selected-slot summary; the right column (≈8/12 width) acts as the primary interaction area, stacking the availability calendar, time-slot list, guest count form, and booking initiation control in a clear top-to-bottom flow. Invisible logic components (state manager and loader) are visually tied to, but not separate from, the calendar area.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport for the full-width header bar. Below it, place the main content band with generous top margin (≈48–64px) before the two-column section begins. Within the main content, the left column occupies around one-third of the width with shorter line lengths, while the right column takes about two-thirds of the width and extends slightly deeper vertically due to the interactive elements. Each stacked block within the right column (calendar, filter + slots, guest count form, CTA) is separated by 16–32px vertical spacing. Horizontal padding inside the main band is constrained to a centered max-width (e.g., 960–1200px), with side gutters of ≈24–32px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious feel: inputs and interactive elements are vertically dense enough to feel like a single cohesive flow, but each retains clear separation (≈8–16px between form elements and list items). The left column text and summary remain light and airy with short paragraphs and comfortable line height. The calendar and slot list are allowed to be visually substantial but not cramped, with enough whitespace around them to preserve the calm, editorial aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first engages with the colored fixed header and centered navigation, with an active underline under “Availability & Booking.” From there it drops down to the left-column page heading and short explanation. The primary attention then shifts to the larger, right-hand interaction column: first the calendar at the top, then the evening filter toggle, then the vertical list of available time slots, followed by the guest-count form, and finally the prominent booking initiation control at the bottom. The selected-slot summary in the left column acts as a secondary anchor and check-point, revisited after the user chooses a slot and enters guests. Overall, the layout encourages a gentle left-to-right, then top-to-bottom reading and interaction path.

layout.overall_description: Below a fixed, full-width header with a muted accent color and centered navigation, the Availability & Booking page opens into a calm, white main content band centered within the viewport. A generous strip of whitespace separates the header from the content, then the layout splits into two columns within a constrained max-width container. On the left, a narrow column holds a clean, dark “Availability & Booking” heading and a brief explanatory paragraph, followed by a tidy summary card that reflects the user’s selected date, time, and guest count. On the right, a wider column dominates the page, beginning with a full-width monthly availability calendar aligned horizontally with the left heading. Directly beneath it, a subtle evening filter toggle sits above a vertical list of individual time slots for the selected day, each evenly spaced to create a readable rhythm. Below the slots, a compact guest-count form continues the vertical flow, and at the bottom of this column a prominent, full-width booking button anchors the interaction. All elements are framed by consistent 8px-based spacing, ample margins, and balanced typography, producing a professional, editorial feel where the right-hand booking flow is clearly primary, while the left-hand column provides context and confirmation without competing for attention.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-manager component has no visible UI and therefore occupies no explicit visual space on the page. Conceptually, it is attached to the availability-calendar area in the right column, controlling the active date and URL parameters. Visually, any effect it has is perceived through synchronized updates of the calendar, day-slot-list, selected-slot-summary, and guest-count-form. It does not create its own block; instead, it is treated as underlying behavior for the calendar and booking flow, so the layout around the calendar remains clean and uninterrupted.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering logic component; behaves as an invisible layer that updates state in response to URL changes without affecting layout or occupying space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-fetcher component itself is not a separate block but manifests as a slim loading state within the footprint of the availability-calendar in the right column. When loading, a subtle overlay covers the calendar area: a semi-transparent white or light-muted layer fills the same rectangular region as the calendar, centered within the right column, with a small spinner or loading indicator positioned at the vertical and horizontal center of that rectangle. The overlay respects the existing padding and margins of the calendar, leaving the spacing around it unchanged. Once data is loaded, the overlay disappears, and the calendar grid is fully visible again without any layout shift.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the availability-calendar area; uses absolute positioning within the calendar container to avoid reflow and maintains the calendar’s size and position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability calendar is the first and largest interactive element in the right column, positioned near the top of the main content band. Horizontally, it spans the full width of the right column (≈8/12 of the total content width), with consistent inner padding around the calendar grid to match the form-field padding from the design DNA (≈12–16px). Vertically, it occupies roughly 25–35% of the visible right column height, enough to show an entire month with clear day cells and subtle spacing between weeks. The top edge of the calendar aligns roughly with the baseline of the left column’s page heading, creating a visual link across columns. A comfortable vertical gap of ≈24–32px separates the bottom of the calendar from the elements beneath it (starting with the evening-filter-toggle), preserving the moderate, airy density of the mockup. The calendar sits on a plain white background with minimal outlines or dividers, relying on subtle grid lines and typography to stay consistent with the minimalist, editorial look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it scrolls with the page content. Its width responds to the overall content container (e.g., shrinking proportionally on smaller screens while maintaining full-column width).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The evening filter toggle is a compact horizontal control placed directly beneath the availability-calendar within the same right column. It sits in the space about 24px below the calendar’s bottom edge, acting as a subtle sub-header for the list of time slots. It is right-aligned within the right column’s width, occupying roughly 20–30% of the column horizontally, so that it feels lightweight and does not visually dominate. The control itself follows the spacing system with around 8px vertical padding and 16px horizontal padding around its label and switch or pill element. The left side of this horizontal row remains mostly empty white space, which reinforces the minimalist feel and keeps the focus on the slots that appear just below. A small gap of ≈12–16px separates the toggle from the top of the day-slot-list, visually pairing them as a unit.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the right column’s flow; it maintains its alignment relative to the right column width and scrolls with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The day-slot-list is positioned immediately below the evening-filter-toggle in the right column, spanning the full width of that column. It appears as a vertical stack of time-slot items, each slot rendered as a single-row pill or card with a subtle border or background, consistent padding (≈8–12px vertically and 12–16px horizontally), and 8–12px vertical spacing between items. The list extends downward and visually occupies a significant vertical portion of the right column, roughly 30–40% of the main content height when many slots are present. There is a clear top margin (≈12–16px) separating the first slot from the filter toggle, and a larger margin (≈24–32px) separating the last slot from the guest-count-form below. When the list becomes long, it continues down the page, preserving the top-to-bottom reading flow; no separate scroll container is used by default, so the page scrolls normally, maintaining the simple editorial rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level list within the right column’s flow; it expands vertically as needed and pushes subsequent components downward. On smaller screens, it remains full-width relative to the content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guest-count-form sits in the right column directly below the day-slot-list, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 24–32px to signify a new step in the booking process. It spans the full width of the right column, with its inner fields arranged in a single vertical stack similar to the form in the original mockup. Each field (e.g., guest count input, possibly event type or a simple note) occupies the full available width, with 8–12px of vertical padding and 12–16px horizontal padding inside the input, and 12–16px vertical spacing between fields. Labels are aligned left above each input, maintaining a clean, straight left edge. The overall form block is moderately compact but surrounded by enough whitespace so it does not feel cramped, occupying roughly 15–25% of the right column’s vertical space depending on field count. A slightly larger margin of ≈24px is placed below the final input before the booking-initiation-controller, visually grouping them while still making the call to action distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width form section within the right column; it stretches to match the column width and scrolls with the page. On narrower viewports, the fields remain stacked and full-width, preserving the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The selected-slot-summary is housed in the left column beneath the main page heading and introductory copy. After a top heading (e.g., “Availability & Booking”) and a short paragraph spaced by ≈16–24px, the summary appears as a tidy card or panel occupying roughly 70–80% of the left column’s width and centered within that column. It begins about 24–32px below the descriptive text, using a light border or subtle shaded background to distinguish it from the rest of the white column while keeping the minimalist style. Inside, key details—selected date, time window, and current guest count—are arranged in a small vertical stack with 8px line spacing, and modest padding of 16–24px around the content. Vertically, the summary card fills around 25–35% of the left column’s height in a typical state, leaving generous white space above and below, preserving the airy, editorial look. When no slot is selected, this panel can show a calm placeholder message while maintaining the same overall dimensions so the left column remains visually stable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as a block within the left column’s flow. Optionally, it can become sticky within the left column on taller pages, remaining visible as the user scrolls through the right column’s calendar and slot list, while respecting a top offset below the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-initiation-controller is the final element in the right column’s stack, placed directly beneath the guest-count-form. It appears as a prominent primary button or compact control group that spans most or all of the right column width—typically 80–100%—and is horizontally aligned with the left edge of the form fields, reinforcing the vertical rhythm. A clear margin of ≈24–32px separates it from the last form field, providing breathing room and emphasizing its importance as the next step. Vertically, the button height follows standard form control sizing (e.g., ≈40–48px tall), with ample internal padding and strong contrast against the white background to make it the main visual anchor at the bottom of the booking flow. Below the controller, an additional 32–48px of whitespace before the end of the content band prevents the layout from feeling cramped at the bottom of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static component at the bottom of the right column’s content flow; it scrolls naturally with the page. On very small screens, it can expand to full width to maintain prominence and tap-target size.

name: Booking Details
title: Booking Details
description: Collects event details and organizer contact information for the selected catering booking slot, and sends them for review before showing the booking summary.

core_features: Read the bookingId from the URL and load the current booking draft using getBookingDetailsForReview.
Display a read-only summary of the selected date, time, and guest count from the loaded Booking.
Provide a form to capture or edit event-specific fields (eventName and eventType) and organizer contact fields (contactName, contactPhone, contactEmail) mapped to the Booking model.
Validate required fields (at least contactName and contactPhone, plus any additional constraints defined by business rules) before submission.
Submit the event and contact details using submitBookingDetails and handle success and error responses.
On successful submission, navigate the user to booking_summary.html with the appropriate bookingId in the URL.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: First-time visit to Booking Details after choosing availability
1. User arrives at booking_details.html with a bookingId in the URL query string.
2. Page reads bookingId from the URL and triggers getBookingDetailsForReview(bookingId).
3. While loading, the slot summary and form are disabled or show a loading state.
4. On successful response, the page shows the selected slot summary (date, startTime, endTime, guestCount) from the Booking.
5. Any existing eventName, eventType, contactName, contactPhone, and contactEmail from the Booking (if present) prefill the form fields.
6. User fills in or adjusts eventName and eventType.
7. User enters contactName and contactPhone and optionally contactEmail.
8. User clicks the primary action button (e.g., Continue / Review Booking), which triggers form validation.
9. If validation passes, submitBookingDetails is called with bookingId and current form values.
10. While submission is in progress, the button is disabled and a submitting state is shown.
11. If submitBookingDetails returns success: true, the page navigates to booking_summary.html?bookingId=<booking.id or original bookingId>.
12. If submitBookingDetails returns success: false or an error occurs, an error message is shown and the user remains on this page to correct inputs or retry.

Workflow 2: Returning to edit booking details
1. User revisits booking_details.html using an existing bookingId in the URL.
2. Page again calls getBookingDetailsForReview(bookingId).
3. The existing Booking data, including previously saved eventName, eventType, and contact fields, prepopulate the form.
4. User updates any of the fields and clicks the primary action button.
5. Page validates the updated data and calls submitBookingDetails with the same bookingId and new values.
6. On success, user is redirected to booking_summary.html with bookingId in the URL, showing an updated summary.
7. On failure, the page shows the returned message and keeps the form editable for corrections or retry.

Workflow 3: Handling missing or invalid bookingId
1. User reaches booking_details.html without a bookingId parameter or with an invalid bookingId format.
2. Page detects that bookingId is absent or invalid and does not attempt to call getBookingDetailsForReview.
3. A page-level error is shown indicating that the booking could not be loaded.
4. The form and submit actions are disabled because there is no valid booking draft to attach details to.
5. Optionally, the page can provide navigation back to the availability step (navigation target is outside the scope of this page definition).
interactions: On page load, read bookingId from the URL query string and, if valid, trigger getBookingDetailsForReview(bookingId).
While getBookingDetailsForReview is in progress, display a loading indicator and prevent form submission.
After getBookingDetailsForReview succeeds, bind the returned Booking data to the slot summary and form fields (pre-filling any existing eventName, eventType, and contact fields).
If getBookingDetailsForReview fails (e.g., network error or booking not found), show a page-level error and disable the form and submit button.
On change of eventName, update the in-memory form state for eventName.
On change of eventType (e.g., select/dropdown or radio control), update the in-memory form state for eventType, ensuring the value matches the Booking.eventType enumeration.
On change of contactName, contactPhone, or contactEmail, update the in-memory form state and clear any related field-level validation messages.
On click of the primary action button (e.g., Continue / Review Booking), trigger form submission logic: validate required fields, then call submitBookingDetails if validation passes.
During submitBookingDetails, show a submitting state (e.g., disabling the button) to prevent duplicate submissions.

If submitBookingDetails returns success: true, read booking.id from the response booking object (or fall back to the original bookingId when appropriate) and navigate to booking_summary.html?bookingId=<resolved bookingId>.
If submitBookingDetails returns success: false or an error response, show a form-level error message using the returned message property (if present) and keep user-entered values intact.
Provide a retry action (e.g., a Retry button or re-click of Continue) that re-attempts submitBookingDetails with the current form state after an error, assuming validation still passes.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the bookingId URL parameter. On initial load, the page parses bookingId from the query string (booking_details.html?bookingId=...). If bookingId is present and valid, it is stored in transient page state and used to call getBookingDetailsForReview(bookingId). The result of this call populates a Booking instance in page state. While this Booking is undefined and a request is in-flight, the page is in a 'loading' state and disables user interactions relating to the booking. If the call fails or bookingId is missing, the page enters an 'error' state where the form and submission actions are disabled, and an error banner is shown. Once Booking data is loaded, the page enters a 'ready' state. In this state, the slot summary section reads eventDate, startTime, endTime, and guestCount from the Booking object and displays them read-only. The form fields eventName, eventType, contactName, contactPhone, and contactEmail are bound to an editable form state, initially derived from the Booking's existing values (if any). When the user submits, the page transitions to a 'submitting' state, during which submitBookingDetails is called with the current bookingId and form state. On successful submission, the page reads the booking object returned by submitBookingDetails, extracts booking.id, and then performs a client-side navigation to booking_summary.html, building the URL as booking_summary.html?bookingId=<booking.id>. If booking.id is not available or unchanged, the page uses the original bookingId from the URL. No booking data is persisted via localStorage; navigation and cross-page identity rely solely on the bookingId propagated through URL parameters.

components.functionality: Parses the bookingId from the current page URL query string and exposes it to other components as a read-only state property. Validates that bookingId is present and non-empty; if invalid or absent, sets a page-level error state to block further data loading and form usage.

Uses the bookingId provided by the routing-state-handler to fetch the current booking draft details via getBookingDetailsForReview. Manages loading and error flags for the page. On success, stores the returned Booking object in shared page state for use by the slot summary and form components.

Displays a non-editable summary of the selected booking slot using fields from the loaded Booking: eventDate, startTime, endTime, and guestCount. Reflects the current state of the booking draft so the user can confirm they are entering details for the correct event slot.

Initializes and maintains the editable form state corresponding to Booking.eventName, Booking.eventType, Booking.contactName, Booking.contactPhone, and Booking.contactEmail. On initial Booking load, it pre-fills form fields using values from the Booking (if present). It tracks dirty/pristine status for each field and stores any field-level validation messages.

Presents input controls for eventName, eventType, contactName, contactPhone, and contactEmail, bound to the form-state-manager. It delegates all data updates to the form-state-manager and triggers validation and submission flows via the submission controller. It also shows inline field error messages when supplied by the validation component.

Applies validation rules to the booking details form before submission. Ensures that required fields like contactName and contactPhone are not empty and that eventType (if required by business rules) is set to an allowed enumeration value. Optionally validates contactEmail format when provided. Produces a collection of field-level errors and a form-level validity flag consumed by the form and submission controller.

Displays page-level errors encountered when loading the Booking (via getBookingDetailsForReview) or when submitting Booking details (via submitBookingDetails). Displays the message returned by submitBookingDetails when available, or a generic error message otherwise. Clears the error display when the user edits the form or initiates a retry.

Coordinates form submission for booking details. When the user submits, it first requests validation from the validation-manager. If the form is valid and a Booking is loaded, it calls submitBookingDetails with bookingId and the current form values for eventName, eventType, contactName, contactPhone, and contactEmail. Manages a submitting state flag, handles success and failure responses, updates error state, and triggers navigation on success.

Handles navigation to booking_summary.html after successful submission. Listens for a successful response from submitBookingDetails, extracts the effective bookingId (preferring booking.id from the response Booking, falling back to the URL bookingId if necessary), and updates window location to booking_summary.html?bookingId=<effectiveBookingId>.

Renders the primary call-to-action for the page (e.g., Continue / Review Booking). Its enabled/disabled state reflects the page submission state (disabled while loading or submitting, or when no valid bookingId is present). On click, it triggers the form submission through the submission controller.

Aggregates high-level page state including isLoading, isReady, isSubmitting, hasLoadError, and hasSubmitError. Receives signals from the data-loader and submission-controller and exposes derived flags used to control component behavior (e.g., disabling the form and button during loading or submission).

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a constrained max-width content area centered in the viewport. The left text block occupies roughly 4 columns and the right form block around 8 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width, fixed header bar with centered navigation at the very top; beneath it, a generous band of white space leading into a max-width main content area that sits centered on the page. Within this main content, use a two-column layout: the left column is narrower (about 1/3 of the content width) and contains the page title, a short explanatory paragraph, and the booking slot summary panel; the right column is wider (about 2/3 of the content width) and contains the vertically stacked Booking Details form and its primary action button. The error banner, when present, appears at the top of the main content column, just under the header and above the two-column layout, spanning the full content width.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width, fixed header bar with centered navigation at the very top; beneath it, a generous band of white space leading into a max-width main content area that sits centered on the page. Within this main content, use a two-column layout: the left column is narrower (about 1/3 of the content width) and contains the page title, a short explanatory paragraph, and the booking slot summary panel; the right column is wider (about 2/3 of the content width) and contains the vertically stacked Booking Details form and its primary action button. The error banner, when present, appears at the top of the main content column, just under the header and above the two-column layout, spanning the full content width.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport to the header/navigation region. The main content area sits below with a 48–64px vertical gap from the header to maintain the spacious feel. Inside the main band, constrain the content to a centered max-width (roughly 1040–1200px). Horizontally, dedicate about 35–40% of this width to the left column (title, copy, summary panel) and 60–65% to the right column (form + primary button). Vertically, the form column is allowed to extend lower than the left column if needed. Within the form, fields use full column width with consistent vertical spacing (about 16–24px) between them.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious density overall. The title, description, and summary panel on the left are minimally dense, with ample whitespace around them. The form on the right is more densely packed but still uses clear vertical separation between fields and groups (around 16px between fields and 24–32px between logical sections). The error banner is visually distinct but not oversized, using moderate padding and clear but restrained color to avoid visual clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the header navigation (with a subtle indication that the user is on the Booking flow) down into the main content. The first focal point in the main area is the left-side title ('Booking Details') in dark, high-contrast text, followed by a short descriptive line, then the slot summary panel beneath. From there, attention is pulled to the larger right column where the form begins near the same vertical baseline as the page title. The user’s eye then travels down the form in a clear top-to-bottom reading path, ending on the primary action button.
