layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a constrained max-width content area centered in the viewport. The left text block occupies roughly 4 columns and the right form block around 8 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear flow: a full-width, fixed header with centered navigation at the very top, then a generous band of white main content centered within a max-width container. Inside this content band, a two-column layout mirrors the mockup: the left column (about 4 of 12 columns) holds the tasting list summary (title and brief copy), while the right column (about 8 of 12 columns) holds all interactive list elements—list type toggle, gluten-free tasting set panel, item list / empty state, and the browse-more-dishes link. Data-loading, notifications, and loading states sit above or within these primary regions without breaking the overall grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear flow: a full-width, fixed header with centered navigation at the very top, then a generous band of white main content centered within a max-width container. Inside this content band, a two-column layout mirrors the mockup: the left column (about 4 of 12 columns) holds the tasting list summary (title and brief copy), while the right column (about 8 of 12 columns) holds all interactive list elements—list type toggle, gluten-free tasting set panel, item list / empty state, and the browse-more-dishes link. Data-loading, notifications, and loading states sit above or within these primary regions without breaking the overall grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport is occupied by the fixed header. Below it, a 48–64px vertical gap separates the header from the main content band. The max-width content area is centered and uses a 12-column grid: the left descriptive column occupies ~1/3 of the width, the right interactive column ~2/3. In the right column, the gluten-free tasting set and item list occupy most of the vertical space, with clear section separations (32–48px) and a bottom area reserved for the browse-more-dishes link. The notification banner spans full width just below the header when present, slightly compressing the remaining vertical space.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a calm, moderately spacious density: individual elements (toggle, panel, rows) are vertically spaced by 16–24px, with 32–48px between larger sections (summary block vs. main list). The left summary content uses short, readable line lengths and 1–2 compact paragraphs. The right column presents list content in a slightly denser but still airy vertical stack, with item rows clearly separated by padding rather than heavy dividers, preserving the minimalist, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the colored header bar and centered navigation (with an active underline on the Tasting List / Favorites item), down to the left-column summary heading, then immediately across to the top of the right-column controls (list-type toggle and gluten-free tasting set). From there, the eye follows a strong vertical path down through the grouped panel and the list of dishes, ending at the browse-more-dishes link. Any notification banner appears just below the header and is seen before the main content; loading indicators appear centered within the right column, briefly becoming the primary focal point during asynchronous operations.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed, full-width header with centered navigation, a wide white content band sits in the middle of the viewport, framed by generous left and right margins. Within this band, the page is split into two columns: on the left, a compact summary area with the page title and a short explanation anchors the experience and mirrors the editorial copy block from the mockup. On the right, a taller, more visually dominant column presents a clean stack of interactive elements: a subtle list-type toggle near the top, a refined gluten-free tasting set panel directly beneath it, followed by a vertically scrolling list of favorite dishes. Each row in the list spans the right column’s width with comfortable padding and clear alignment, maintaining a consistent vertical rhythm. When there are no items, a centered empty-state message in the right column replaces the list. A simple text-based link at the bottom invites the user to browse more dishes, aligning with the minimalist, upscale aesthetic. Across the top of the content band, a notification banner can appear just under the header, and loading indicators briefly take over the central area of the right column without disturbing the underlying grid or spatial balance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is an invisible structural layer that wraps the main content in the right column. It occupies the same horizontal footprint as the right column (approximately 8 of 12 columns, aligned to the right of the summary column) and extends vertically to cover the area where the gluten-free tasting set, item list, and empty-state message would appear. Depending on loading or error state, it toggles visibility of the loading indicator, notification banner, main list content, or empty state, but does not introduce new visual geometry of its own.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal block container in the main content flow, wrapping and controlling the spatial presence of the right-column components without changing their alignment or the two-column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The list-type toggle is positioned near the top of the right column, aligned horizontally with the main summary heading on the left. It sits flush with the left edge of the right column content, about 48–64px below the header (or notification banner if visible), with 16–24px of whitespace above and below. The toggle itself occupies only the necessary width for its labels, appearing as a horizontal pill or segmented control; it does not span the full column width, leaving comfortable negative space on its right to maintain the minimalist, editorial look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains statically positioned at the top of the right column content, scrolling away with the rest of the page content; it does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tasting-list summary block sits at the top of the main content band in the left column, mirroring the position of the hero heading in the mockup. It begins approximately 48–64px below the bottom edge of the header (or notification banner), aligned to the left within a column that occupies roughly 4 of the 12 grid columns. The main title appears at the top of this block, with 8–16px of space before a short 1–2 sentence paragraph. The vertical top baseline of this block aligns closely with the top of the list-type toggle and gluten-free tasting set in the right column, reinforcing a clean horizontal rhythm across the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element anchored in the left column of the main content band, scrolling normally with the page and setting the visual starting point for the content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gluten-free tasting set appears as a prominent, full-width panel within the right column, directly below the list-type toggle with about 16–24px of vertical separation. It spans nearly the entire width of the right column (about 8 grid columns), with an internal padding of roughly 16–24px on all sides to create a card-like feel without heavy decoration. Its top edge sits at roughly the same vertical level as the tasting-list summary title on the left, making these two elements feel like paired anchors. The panel’s content (title, description, items, and controls) is stacked vertically with clear spacing, creating the sense of a featured group within the user’s favorites.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the right column; it expands vertically to fit its contents and pushes the item list downward as needed but does not overlap or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tasting-list item list is positioned directly beneath the gluten-free tasting set panel in the right column, separated by about 24–32px to signal a shift from featured set to general favorites. It occupies the full width of the right column and extends downward as a vertical stack of rows. Each row is aligned to the same left and right margins as the gluten-free panel, maintaining a flush vertical edge on both sides. The list can scroll vertically with the page; if the list is long, the right column becomes the primary scrolling content zone while the left summary remains relatively compact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack within the right column, growing in height according to the number of items. It scrolls normally with the page rather than using an internal scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each tasting-list item row is a horizontal band within the item list, spanning the full width of the right column’s content area. Rows have generous vertical padding (approximately 12–16px top and bottom) and horizontal padding that aligns with the panel and column gutters (about 16–24px). The dish name is aligned toward the left, with secondary details and actions (e.g., view details, remove) aligned to the right or arranged in a subtle multi-column layout within the row, while still respecting the overall grid. There is 8–12px of whitespace between successive rows, possibly indicated by a very light divider or only by spacing to maintain the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Each row is a block-level element in the vertical list stack. Rows maintain consistent height based on content and spacing and respond to hover/focus states without changing their spatial footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the tasting list is empty, the empty-state message replaces both the gluten-free tasting set panel and the item list within the right column. It occupies the same horizontal footprint as those components (full width of the right column) but uses a more centered, spacious composition. The message block is placed slightly below the vertical baseline of the summary on the left, with roughly 64–96px of top padding from the main content band’s top edge, giving it breathing room and making it an obvious focal point. Text is center-aligned or softly left-aligned within the column, with any supporting iconography or CTAs stacked vertically and surrounded by generous whitespace to preserve the calm, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally in place of the gluten-free panel and item list, occupying their combined space. It scrolls with the page but, due to additional vertical padding, often appears near the vertical center of the main content area on typical screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The browse-more-dishes link sits near the bottom of the right column’s content, directly below the item list (or empty state) with approximately 32–48px of vertical separation. It is left-aligned with the right column’s content edge, appearing as a simple text link or subtle button that spans only its text width, not the full column. There is comfortable whitespace below it (at least 48px) before the end of the main content band, giving the link room to breathe and making it feel like a natural continuation of the user’s journey rather than a cramped footer element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the right column content area. As the list grows or shrinks, the link moves up or down accordingly and scrolls off-screen naturally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API notification banner appears as a full-width strip directly beneath the fixed header and above the main content band, spanning 100% of the viewport width. Its content is centered within the same max-width container used by the main content, aligning its text and any iconography with the 12-column grid. The banner has moderate vertical padding (about 8–12px) and a subtle tinted background color, ensuring it catches the eye before the main content without feeling heavy. When present, it pushes the entire main content band downward, preserving the consistent 48–64px gap between the bottom of the banner and the top of the tasting-list summary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in the document flow directly under the site header; it is not sticky relative to scrolling but remains visible above the content until dismissed or hidden by state changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator appears within the bounds of the right column, centered both horizontally and vertically within the area reserved for the gluten-free panel and item list. On initial load or during data refreshes, it temporarily replaces that content, leaving the left summary column visible but drawing attention to the center of the right column. Surrounding whitespace is generous—at least 48–64px from the nearest visible elements—so the indicator, whether a spinner or subtle animation, feels deliberate and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed conditionally as an overlay or replacement block within the right column’s content area; it scrolls with the page but typically appears while the user is already near the top of the list area.

name: Quote Request
title: Quote Request
description: Page for requesting a detailed catering quote for a specific package configuration, using preselected package, guest count, add-ons, and event date, and collecting contact details.

core_features: Initialize the quote request context from URL parameters by calling initQuoteRequestFromPackage, loading the selected Package, guest count, selected AddOns, any preselected event date, and estimated price per person range.

Display a read-only summary of the configured package (name, category if provided in the Package model, guest count, selected add-ons, and estimated price range) so the user can verify what they are requesting a quote for.
Provide an event date input (date picker compatible) that is prefilled from the initialized eventDate when available and is required before submitting a quote request.
Provide contact information inputs for contactName, contactEmail, and optional contactPhone, plus an additionalNotes textarea for special requirements or details.

Allow the user to adjust the guestCount for the quote request (starting from the initialized value) and optionally refresh estimated pricing by calling initQuoteRequestFromPackage again with the updated guestCount.
Submit the completed quote request by calling submitQuoteRequest with the current packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, eventDate, and contact information.
Show inline feedback after submission, including success or error messages based on submitQuoteRequest response and, on success, basic QuoteRequest details (such as status and ID if provided).
Handle missing or invalid URL parameters (especially packageId) by preventing form submission and showing an inline error state that prompts the user to navigate back to a package selection page.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Requesting a quote from a package detail page
1. User configures a package on package_detail.html (selects guest count, add-ons, and optionally an event date) and clicks a 'Request Quote' link or button.
2. The site navigates to quote_request.html with URL parameters including packageId and optionally guestCount, selectedAddonIds, and eventDate.
3. On load, the Quote Request page parses the URL parameters and calls initQuoteRequestFromPackage with packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, and eventDate.
4. The page receives the Package, selected AddOns, guestCount, eventDate, and estimatedPricePerPersonMin/Max, and populates the quote summary panel and form defaults.
5. The user reviews the summary to confirm the package, add-ons, and guest count.
6. The user adjusts the event date if needed, and fills in contactName, contactEmail, contactPhone (optional), and additionalNotes (optional).
7. Optionally, the user edits the guestCount field and clicks an 'Update estimate' control, which re-calls initQuoteRequestFromPackage with the new guestCount while keeping the same packageId, selectedAddonIds, and current eventDate, refreshing the estimated price range in the summary.
8. When ready, the user clicks the 'Request Quote' submit button.
9. The page validates required fields (packageId in context, guestCount, eventDate, contactName, contactEmail) and, if valid, calls submitQuoteRequest with the current form values and configuration.
10. On success, the page displays an inline confirmation message using the success flag, message, and returned QuoteRequest data; the form is locked against duplicate submission, and the URL may be updated to include the quoteRequestId and a status flag.
11. On failure, the page displays the error message from submitQuoteRequest and allows the user to correct any issues and resubmit.

Workflow 2: Handling missing or invalid packageId
1. User or external system navigates to quote_request.html without a packageId URL parameter or with an invalid packageId.
2. On load, the page attempts to parse packageId and detects that it is missing or not usable.
3. The page does not call initQuoteRequestFromPackage and instead shows a blocking inline error in the main content explaining that a package must be selected before requesting a quote.
4. The quote request form fields and submit button are disabled because submitQuoteRequest requires a valid packageId.
5. The user can click a navigation link (for example, back to index.html or package listing pages) to select a package and restart the flow with correct URL parameters.

Workflow 3: Reloading the page after a successful quote submission
1. User has successfully submitted a quote request; the page receives a QuoteRequest object and success=true from submitQuoteRequest.
2. The page displays a success state and may update the URL (via query parameters like quoteRequestId and status=submitted) without resubmitting.
3. If the user reloads the page, the URL still includes packageId and other configuration parameters, so the page can call initQuoteRequestFromPackage again to show the summary context.
4. The submission feedback component, reading the updated URL state (for example, status=submitted and quoteRequestId), shows that a quote has already been submitted and can hide or disable the form to prevent accidental duplicate submissions.
interactions: On page load: Parse URL parameters (packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, eventDate). If packageId is present and valid, call initQuoteRequestFromPackage with these values to initialize the page state; otherwise, render an error state and disable the form.

On URL change (if the application supports internal navigation that modifies query parameters without full reload): Re-parse URL parameters and, when packageId is present, re-call initQuoteRequestFromPackage to refresh the summary and default form values.

On guestCount field change: Update the in-page guestCount state bound to the form; if the user explicitly activates an 'Update estimate' control, call initQuoteRequestFromPackage again with the new guestCount and the current packageId, selectedAddonIds, and (optional) eventDate to refresh the estimated price per person range.
On eventDate field change: Update the in-page eventDate state; this value will be passed to submitQuoteRequest when the quote is submitted.

On contactName, contactEmail, contactPhone, and additionalNotes input changes: Update the local form state bound to these fields; perform inline validation for required fields (contactName and contactEmail) and email format before enabling submission.

On 'Request Quote' button click (form submit): Run client-side validation (checking required fields, basic formats). If validation passes and packageId is set, call submitQuoteRequest with the current values for packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds (as currently known from initialization), eventDate, contactName, contactEmail, contactPhone, and additionalNotes.

On submitQuoteRequest success response: Set a submissionSuccess flag, store the returned QuoteRequest object in page state, update the inline confirmation message with the response message and QuoteRequest details, optionally disable the form or mark it as submitted, and optionally update the URL query string to reflect submitted state.

On submitQuoteRequest failure response or network error: Set a submissionError flag, display the response message (if provided) or a generic error message, keep the current form values so the user can adjust and retry, and ensure the submit button is re-enabled.

On 'Edit package configuration' link click: Navigate back to package_detail.html (or another configured page) with the current packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, and eventDate included as URL parameters so the user can adjust configuration before returning to the Quote Request page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL query parameters for its initial and persisted state. On load, packageId (string) is required for package-specific quotes; guestCount (number), selectedAddonIds (string of comma-separated IDs), and eventDate (string) are optional but used when present. The page parses these parameters and, when packageId is available, calls initQuoteRequestFromPackage(packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, eventDate). The response populates the in-memory page state: package (Package entity), guestCount (number), selectedAddOns (array of AddOn), eventDate (string), and estimatedPricePerPersonMin/Max (numbers). These values are then bound to the quote summary panel and form defaults. The selectedAddonIds used when calling submitQuoteRequest are derived from the initialized state (for example, by reusing the original selectedAddonIds from the URL or a string reconstructed from the selectedAddOns list); the page does not fetch or modify add-ons beyond what initQuoteRequestFromPackage returns. After a successful submitQuoteRequest call, the page can use history.pushState or a similar mechanism to update the current URL's query parameters (for example, adding quoteRequestId set to quoteRequest.id and status=submitted while preserving packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, and eventDate). On subsequent reloads, the page again parses URL parameters, reinitializes via initQuoteRequestFromPackage (using packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, eventDate), and, based on presence of status=submitted or quoteRequestId, shows a submitted state and may disable or hide the form to prevent duplicate submissions. All transient UI states (validation errors, loading flags, and submission flags) are kept in memory only and are not stored in localStorage; persistent configuration and submission context are always derived from the URL parameters and API responses from initQuoteRequestFromPackage and submitQuoteRequest.

components.functionality: Parses URL query parameters (packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, eventDate, and optionally status, quoteRequestId) and manages initialization of the quote request context by calling initQuoteRequestFromPackage. It exposes normalized state to other components, including the loaded Package, selected AddOns, initial guestCount, initial eventDate, and price estimate range. It also manages updating the URL query parameters after successful submission to reflect the submitted state.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: On initial page load, read window.location.search, extract packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, and eventDate. If packageId is present, call initQuoteRequestFromPackage(packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, eventDate). Store the returned package, guestCount, selectedAddOns, eventDate, estimatedPricePerPersonMin, and estimatedPricePerPersonMax in shared state accessible to other components. If packageId is missing, set an error state that other components can use to disable themselves.

onUrlChange: If the application updates query parameters without reloading (for example, after submission), re-parse the URL. If packageId is still present, optionally re-call initQuoteRequestFromPackage with the latest parameters to keep the summary in sync. Also update any submission status flags based on parameters like status and quoteRequestId.

onEstimateUpdateRequest: When downstream components (e.g., the quote-request-form) request a refresh of the price estimate, call initQuoteRequestFromPackage again with the current packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, and eventDate from state, then update the shared state with the new price estimates.

onSubmissionSuccess: After submitQuoteRequest succeeds, receive the returned QuoteRequest from the form component and update the shared state with it. Optionally update URL parameters (e.g., adding quoteRequestId and status=submitted) using history APIs without reloading the page, so that a refresh does not re-submit the form but preserves the context.

components.functionality: Displays a non-editable summary of the package configuration for which the quote will be requested. It uses the Package and AddOn data returned from initQuoteRequestFromPackage to show the package name and relevant attributes, the current guestCount, the list of selected add-ons, and the estimated price per person range. It also shows the currently selected eventDate if available. If the initialization fails or packageId is missing, the component shows an inline error and hides the summary content.
event_handlers: onInitStateChange: When the page-state-manager updates the initialized context (package, selectedAddOns, guestCount, eventDate, estimate range), re-render the summary fields. If there is an error state (for example, missing packageId or failed initQuoteRequestFromPackage call), display an error message and hide or dim the summary.

onEditConfigurationClick: When the user clicks an 'Edit configuration' link or button in the summary, navigate to package_detail.html with the current packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, and eventDate encoded into the URL query string, allowing the user to adjust their configuration before returning to the Quote Request page. This interaction reads the current values from the shared state managed by the page-state-manager.

components.functionality: Collects user inputs required to submit a quote request, including eventDate (if not fixed), guestCount (initially populated from initQuoteRequestFromPackage), contactName, contactEmail, optional contactPhone, and additionalNotes. It validates required fields and, upon submission, calls submitQuoteRequest with the combined configuration (packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, eventDate) and contact details. It also provides an explicit control to refresh price estimates by asking the page-state-manager to re-call initQuoteRequestFromPackage when the guestCount changes.
event_handlers: onFormMount: Read initial packageId, guestCount, selectedAddonIds, and eventDate from the shared state provided by the page-state-manager (based on initQuoteRequestFromPackage). Initialize form fields: guestCount (editable), eventDate (editable), contactName (empty), contactEmail (empty), contactPhone (empty), additionalNotes (empty). If there is no valid packageId or initialization error, disable all inputs and show a message indicating that a package must be selected first.

onGuestCountChange: Update the form's internal guestCount value as the user types or selects a new number. Run basic validation (for example, ensuring the value is positive). Do not automatically call initQuoteRequestFromPackage on every keystroke; instead, prepare the value to be used when the user explicitly requests an estimate update or submits the form.

onUpdateEstimateClick: When the user clicks an 'Update estimate' control, invoke an event to the page-state-manager (onEstimateUpdateRequest) with the updated guestCount. The page-state-manager then calls initQuoteRequestFromPackage with the updated guestCount and existing packageId, selectedAddonIds, and eventDate. The form listens to subsequent state changes to display updated estimated price per person values in the summary component.

onEventDateChange: Update the form's internal eventDate value from the date input. Perform basic validation to ensure a value is present before submission. This value will be passed to submitQuoteRequest as the eventDate parameter.
onContactNameChange: Update the contactName value and mark the field as valid/invalid depending on whether it is non-empty.
onContactEmailChange: Update the contactEmail value and perform basic format validation (for example, presence of '@'). Mark the field as valid/invalid accordingly.
onContactPhoneChange: Update the optional contactPhone value; allow it to be empty but validate format if provided.
onAdditionalNotesChange: Update the additionalNotes value as the user types; this field is freeform and optional.

onSubmit: When the user submits the form (e.g., clicking 'Request Quote'), perform client-side validation: ensure that packageId from state is present, guestCount is valid, eventDate is set, contactName and contactEmail are non-empty and correctly formatted. If validation fails, prevent submission and show inline error messages. If validation passes, call submitQuoteRequest with the following parameters: packageId from state, guestCount from the form, selectedAddonIds derived from the initial configuration (for example, using the original URL string or a recomposed comma-separated string from selectedAddOns), eventDate from the form, contactName, contactEmail, contactPhone, and additionalNotes. While awaiting the response, show a loading state and disable the submit button to prevent duplicate submissions.

onSubmitSuccess: When submitQuoteRequest returns success=true, pass the returned QuoteRequest object and message to the page-state-manager via an onSubmissionSuccess event. Update the form UI to reflect the success state (for example, disable all fields, hide the submit button, and show a confirmation section). Clear or mark internal error states as resolved.

onSubmitError: When submitQuoteRequest returns success=false or an error occurs, display the error message inline, re-enable the submit button, and keep all user-entered values in the form so the user can correct issues and attempt submission again.

components.functionality: Presents the current submission status of the quote request, including success, error, or not-submitted states. On success, it can show details from the QuoteRequest returned by submitQuoteRequest (such as id, status, and eventDate) alongside the API message. On failure, it shows an error message and guidance to retry. It can also respond to URL state indicating a previously submitted quote.
event_handlers: onSubmissionStateChange: Listen for changes in submission-related state from the page-state-manager and quote-request-form. When a new QuoteRequest is available and success was indicated, render a success banner with the message from submitQuoteRequest and any relevant fields from the QuoteRequest (e.g., status, eventDate), and indicate that the request has been received.

onUrlStateChange: When the URL contains parameters indicating a previously submitted quote (for example, quoteRequestId and status=submitted), but the page is freshly loaded, set the banner to show that a quote has already been submitted. This may involve using summary information inferred from the preserved configuration combined with the status flag, without re-calling submitQuoteRequest.

onDismissClick: If the component offers a dismiss interaction for the message, update local UI state to hide the banner while leaving the underlying submission state unchanged; the user can continue interacting with other parts of the page if allowed.

components.functionality: Centralizes logic for displaying field-level and form-level validation messages for the quote request form. It reacts to validation state in the form component and ensures that users receive clear guidance on how to correct invalid or missing data before submitQuoteRequest is called.
event_handlers: onFieldValidationUpdate: When the quote-request-form updates the validation state of individual fields (guestCount, eventDate, contactName, contactEmail, contactPhone), update the error or help messages displayed next to those fields.

onFormValidationError: When the user attempts to submit and the form determines that required data is missing or invalid, display a form-level message summarizing what needs to be corrected, and optionally scroll the viewport to the first invalid field.

onFormValidationClear: When the form becomes valid (for example, after the user corrects errors), clear or hide the corresponding validation messages so that the user knows they can proceed to submission.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a constrained max-width content area centered in the viewport. The left text block occupies roughly 4 columns and the right form block around 8 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width, fixed-height header with centered navigation at the very top (global, not part of the components list). Below that, a single main content band sits on a white background, centered within a constrained max-width. Inside this band, there is a two-column layout: the left column (roughly 1/3 of the content width) is used for the quote summary and brief explanatory text, while the right column (roughly 2/3 of the width) contains the quote request form. The form column is visually heavier and is the primary action area. Within the right column, the status banner and validation helper align vertically with the form fields, maintaining a stacked, linear progression from top to bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width, fixed-height header with centered navigation at the very top (global, not part of the components list). Below that, a single main content band sits on a white background, centered within a constrained max-width. Inside this band, there is a two-column layout: the left column (roughly 1/3 of the content width) is used for the quote summary and brief explanatory text, while the right column (roughly 2/3 of the width) contains the quote request form. The form column is visually heavier and is the primary action area. Within the right column, the status banner and validation helper align vertically with the form fields, maintaining a stacked, linear progression from top to bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport is reserved for the full-width header bar. The main content area sits below, separated by about 48–64px of vertical whitespace. The main content band itself is centered and constrained to a max-width of around 1040–1200px. Within that, the left column (quote summary) uses roughly 4 of 12 grid columns (about 30–35% of the width), and the right column (form, status, validation) uses about 8 of 12 grid columns (about 65–70%). The form fields span nearly the full width of the right column, while the summary text maintains shorter line lengths and more generous side padding. Vertical spacing between major sections (summary vs form, header vs content band) is in the 48–64px range; spacing between individual form fields and within the summary panel is in the 16–24px range, matching the base 8px system.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is moderately spacious with deliberate breathing room. The quote summary panel uses short headings and compact bullet/line items with ample white space around them, resulting in low information density and easy scanning. The form fields are stacked with consistent vertical gaps (about 16–24px), creating a clear vertical rhythm without feeling cramped. The status banner and validation helper are slim, unobtrusive blocks that occupy minimal height and do not crowd the form; they are visually distinct but not oversized. Overall density is calm and professional, with no element feeling overly compressed.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first meets the colored header bar and reads the navigation from left to right. The active navigation item for the quote/quote request section is subtly indicated (e.g., underline), orienting the user. From there, the eye drops to the main content band: first to the left column’s heading (e.g., 'Your Quote Summary') and short explanatory text, then quickly to the right column where the vertical stack of form fields dominates. Within the right column, the reading order is top-down: any page-level title, followed by the status banner (when present), then the validation helper, and then the form fields in sequence. The form’s strong vertical rhythm encourages a simple, top-to-bottom completion flow, while the left summary panel remains in peripheral view as a stable reference.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a refined, modern quote request screen with a fixed, full-width header on top and a serene white content canvas below. The main content is centered and constrained, floating within generous lateral margins. Inside this canvas, a two-column structure anchors the layout: on the left, a narrower vertical panel summarizes the selected catering package, guest count, event date, and add-ons using brief text and tight grouping; on the right, a taller, more prominent column houses the quote request form. Both columns start from nearly the same vertical baseline, creating a clean horizontal alignment under the header. The right column draws the most attention, with a subtle status banner and validation helper stacked above a full-height vertical progression of input fields. The overall feel is minimal and editorial: plenty of white space, clean type, and a disciplined grid that guides the eye from the navigation, to the summary, and then down through the form in a natural, uninterrupted flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates purely at the application state level and does not render visible UI. In layout terms, it occupies no visual space and has no direct presence in the grid. Conceptually, it is associated with the main content band, managing URL parameters for the selected package, guest count, add-ons, and date. It should not introduce any padding, margins, or spacing; all visible spacing and structure come from the surrounding layout and the other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, non-layout component; exists outside the rendered flow without affecting positioning, alignment, or spacing of other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote summary panel is positioned in the left column of the main content band, starting near the top of the band, aligned horizontally with the top of the form column on the right. It occupies roughly 30–35% of the total content width (about 4 of 12 columns in a centered grid) and stretches vertically just enough to contain its content (typically less tall than the form on the right). The panel begins about 48–64px below the bottom of the header, with 24–32px of vertical spacing above any internal heading (e.g., 'Your Quote Summary') and the first line of summary content. Left and right padding inside the panel are in the 16–24px range, creating short, editorial-feeling line lengths. Summary items (package name, guest count, event date, add-ons, estimated base price) are stacked vertically with consistent 8–16px gaps, maintaining a light, airy density. The right edge of this panel aligns with the vertical grid line that separates the left 4 columns from the right 8 columns, creating a crisp boundary between summary and form. On taller viewports, the summary appears as a stable vertical block that stops before the page fold, while the form extends further downward on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element inside the left column of the main content grid. It scrolls with the page and does not use sticky or fixed behavior by default, though it may be visually styled as a subtle card without altering its flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote request form anchors the right column of the main content area and is the dominant vertical element on the page. It starts at the same vertical line as the top of the quote summary panel, with its left edge aligned to the grid so that it occupies roughly 65–70% of the total content width (about 8 of 12 grid columns). Within this column, the form fields stretch nearly full width, leaving a modest 16–24px of internal left and right padding so that the inputs do not touch the column edges. At the top of this column, a concise form title (e.g., 'Request Your Detailed Quote') sits with 16–24px spacing above the first functional element (status banner if present, otherwise the first field). Below the title, the form fields—contact details, event details, and any follow-up questions—are stacked in a single column with consistent vertical spacing of 16–24px between each field group. Each input has internal padding of about 8–12px vertically and 12–16px horizontally, reinforcing a calm but efficient density. The submit button and any secondary actions sit at the bottom of this stack, aligned left with the inputs and separated from the last field by approximately 24–32px. This entire column extends vertically further than the summary panel, giving the impression of a tall, elegant form block that defines the main reading path from top to bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the right column of the main content grid. It scrolls naturally with the page and maintains its two-column relationship to the summary panel on wider viewports; on narrower breakpoints it would stack below the summary, but in the desktop layout it remains fixed in the right 8-column span.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission feedback component appears as a slim, full-width banner within the right column, positioned directly above the quote request form fields and below any form title or introductory line. When visible, it spans the entire width of the form column, aligning its left and right edges with the form’s inputs. Vertically, it adds roughly 40–64px of height (depending on content) between the title and the first field: 8–16px of padding above and below the feedback text, plus 16px of vertical margin separating it from the title above and the first input below. The banner uses a muted yet noticeable background tone that contrasts gently with the white page while staying within the sophisticated palette, ensuring it is clearly seen but not overwhelming. It contributes to the vertical rhythm by reading as the first step in the form’s downward flow: title → feedback banner (if present) → validation helper (if present) → form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional static block within the right column. It only appears after a submission attempt or specific status change and, when present, pushes the form fields downward without overlapping them. It scrolls with the rest of the form content and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation messages component is positioned in the right column, immediately below the submission feedback banner (when present) or directly below the form title if the feedback banner is not shown. It takes the form of a compact, text-focused helper block that spans the same width as the form fields, aligning with their left and right edges. Vertically, it adds a slim band of content—approximately 24–40px high for brief messages—using 8–12px vertical padding and 12–16px horizontal padding inside. There is a small 8–12px gap above and below it, so it feels connected to the form but not crowded. Its content might list general validation guidance or a brief summary of errors after an attempted submission. In addition to this central helper block, field-level errors are surfaced inline near their respective fields; however, the layout anchor for this component is as an early element in the form’s vertical stack, helping users understand issues before they engage with individual inputs. In the overall hierarchy, it reads as a subtle, text-dense slice between the status banner and the first form field, maintaining the form’s clear top-to-bottom flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, conditional block within the right column that appears and disappears based on validation state. It does not overlay content or change column structure; it simply expands or collapses the vertical spacing above the form fields, moving them downward when active.

name: About
title: About
description: Displays dynamically loaded information about the catering business (story, expertise, and service philosophy) and provides navigation to contact and package/menu pages.

core_features: Load and display About page content (heading, subheading, body, specializations, awards and certifications, service highlights) via the getAboutPageContent interface.
Present the company’s areas of specialization (e.g., corporate events, weddings, parties, coffee breaks) in a structured list based on the specializations data.
Present quality indicators such as awards, certifications, and service highlights in structured lists based on awardsAndCertifications and serviceHighlights data.
Provide a clear navigation action to contact.html with a prefilled subject parameter for general inquiries.
Provide a clear navigation action to packages.html with a categoryId parameter set to show packages for all events.
user_workflows: View About Content: 1) User navigates to about.html. 2) On page load, the page calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters. 3) The returned heading, subheading, and body are rendered in the main content area. 4) The returned arrays specializations, awardsAndCertifications, and serviceHighlights are rendered as structured lists. 5) User reads through the information to understand the company’s story, expertise, and service philosophy.

Navigate to Contact for General Inquiry: 1) While on about.html, user decides to reach out with a question. 2) User clicks the "Contact Us" action element in the About page. 3) The click handler constructs the URL contact.html?subject=General%20Inquiry. 4) Browser navigates to the constructed URL. 5) On the Contact page (out of scope here), the subject parameter can be used to prefill or contextualize the inquiry.

Navigate to Packages/Menus from About: 1) While on about.html, user wants to see available menus or packages. 2) User clicks the "Explore Our Menus" (or equivalent) action element. 3) The click handler constructs the URL packages.html?categoryId=all_events. 4) Browser navigates to the constructed URL, where all event categories can be shown according to the categoryId parameter.
interactions: Initial Content Load: On DOMContentLoaded or equivalent page initialization event, trigger an asynchronous call to getAboutPageContent. When the promise resolves, update the About content components with the returned data fields. If the call fails, show a generic error state or fallback message via a dedicated component.

Contact Navigation Click: When the user clicks the Contact navigation control, run an onClick handler that prevents any default behavior (if necessary) and sets window.location (or equivalent) to contact.html?subject=General%20Inquiry.
Packages Navigation Click: When the user clicks the Packages/Menus navigation control, run an onClick handler that sets window.location (or equivalent) to packages.html?categoryId=all_events.

Expandable/Collapsible Detail Sections (optional behavior tied to service highlights): If desired, clicking on a service highlight item can toggle detailed text visibility if such detail is provided in the body or structured content. The state of expansion is kept in memory (no URL parameters required) and reset on page reload.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page itself does not consume any URL parameters for its own content; it always loads data via getAboutPageContent without parameters. All page state is ephemeral (in-memory) and reset on reload. Outgoing navigation to other pages uses URL parameters to communicate intent: clicking the Contact action navigates to contact.html with the query parameter subject=General%20Inquiry, and clicking the Packages/Menus action navigates to packages.html with categoryId=all_events. These parameters are composed purely in the click handlers and appended to the target URLs; no localStorage or other client-side persistence is used.

components.functionality: Fetches About page content from the getAboutPageContent interface on page initialization and distributes the data to child components responsible for rendering heading, subheading, body, specializations, awards, and service highlights.
Displays the main heading and optional subheading for the About page as plain text, strictly using data returned from getAboutPageContent.
Renders the primary About body content (story, philosophy, and general description) as a text block, using only the body field provided by getAboutPageContent.

Displays a structured list of the company’s areas of specialization using the specializations array. Each item is rendered as a simple list entry without hardcoded labels, relying entirely on dynamic data.
Displays a list of awards and certifications using the awardsAndCertifications array from getAboutPageContent, to reassure users about quality and expertise.

Displays key service highlights (e.g., reliability, flexibility, dietary accommodations) using the serviceHighlights array, optionally supporting per-item expansion if richer data is provided within the same field.
Provides a dedicated action for users to navigate from the About page to the Contact page, constructing the URL with the subject query parameter set for a general inquiry.
Provides a dedicated action for users to navigate from the About page to the packages listing, constructing the URL with categoryId set to all_events so the target page can show all event packages.
Displays a loading indicator while getAboutPageContent is in progress, hides itself on success, and can show a generic error message if the data load fails.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a constrained max-width content area centered in the viewport. The left text block occupies roughly 4 columns and the right form block around 8 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow: a full-width, fixed header with centered navigation at the very top (shared across the site), followed by a single main content band on a white background. Inside this band, content is organized into a two-column layout on a 12-column grid: the left column (about 4 columns wide) contains the About page heading, the story/body text, and subtle navigation links to Contact and Packages. The right column (about 8 columns wide) houses the dynamically loaded structured content: specializations, service highlights, and awards/certifications, stacked vertically as a tall, cohesive block analogous to the form block in the mockup. The data-loader and loading-state sit conceptually over this main band, controlling what appears until the content is ready.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow: a full-width, fixed header with centered navigation at the very top (shared across the site), followed by a single main content band on a white background. Inside this band, content is organized into a two-column layout on a 12-column grid: the left column (about 4 columns wide) contains the About page heading, the story/body text, and subtle navigation links to Contact and Packages. The right column (about 8 columns wide) houses the dynamically loaded structured content: specializations, service highlights, and awards/certifications, stacked vertically as a tall, cohesive block analogous to the form block in the mockup. The data-loader and loading-state sit conceptually over this main band, controlling what appears until the content is ready.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height and spans 100% width. Below it, there is a generous vertical gap (around 48–64px) before the main content band begins. The main content area is centered, constrained to a max-width (approx. 960–1200px) and uses a 12-column grid: the left column (about 30–35% of the width, ~4 columns) hosts concise text and navigation links; the right column (about 65–70%, ~8 columns) is visually dominant, filled by the stacked data lists. Vertical spacing between main content blocks (heading, body, lists) follows the 24–32px rhythm, with slightly larger breaks (32–48px) between major groupings (e.g., between text area and right-column lists, between each list group).
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout mirrors the mockup’s moderately spacious feel. Text blocks on the left use short paragraphs with comfortable line length, preserving low information density and an editorial tone. On the right, lists are vertically stacked with consistent spacing (16–24px between items, 24–32px between list sections), avoiding crowding while still forming a substantial vertical block. Padding inside any card-like list items follows the input-like padding in the mockup (around 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal), maintaining rhythm and comfort. Overall, there is ample whitespace around and between all elements so the page feels calm and premium rather than busy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the full-width header and navigation, then naturally drops down into the main content band. Within that band, attention starts at the left column with the prominent About heading, then moves into the short body copy that explains the catering story and philosophy. From this focused left column, the user’s gaze is pulled to the right by the larger vertical block of structured information (specializations, service highlights, awards) which echoes the tall form block in the mockup. The right column encourages a top-to-bottom scan: from specializations at the top, through service highlights, and finally awards/certifications at the bottom. The subtle Contact and Packages navigation links beneath the body text offer lateral exits, but are visually understated so they support rather than disrupt the primary vertical reading path. During loading, the loading state occupies the same central band, drawing the eye centrally while hinting at the two-column structure that will appear.

layout.overall_description: Below a fixed, full-width header with centered navigation, the About page opens into a wide, white content band that sits comfortably within the viewport with generous top whitespace. This band is centered and constrained to a max-width, divided into a 12-column grid. On the left, a narrower column holds the 'About' heading and a brief, well-spaced narrative about the catering business, followed by subtle text-style links that invite users to view packages/menus or move to the contact page. On the right, a wider column forms a tall, cohesive block: a structured list of specializations at the top, a list of service highlights beneath it, and a summary of awards and certifications at the bottom. Each section is separated by consistent vertical spacing, with list items styled like lightweight, card-like rows to echo the form-field rhythm from the mockup. The overall effect is modern and editorial: the left side tells the story in a quiet, focused way, while the right side presents concrete proof of expertise and quality in a visually dominant vertical column. When content is loading, a central loading state occupies this band, aligned to the same grid so that the user senses the forthcoming two-column structure even before data appears.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader conceptually wraps the entire main content band beneath the header. Spatially, it corresponds to the full max-width content container centered under the header, spanning the combined area occupied by both the left and right columns. It sits directly below the generous top margin from the header (about 48–64px) and extends downward to cover all about-related content: the heading, body text, lists, and navigation links. When loading is in progress, this loader’s area is effectively occupied by the loading state indicator; when loading is complete, it yields the same space to the actual content components, preserving the overall height and alignment of the two-column layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual wrapper in the normal document flow; it governs when the about-loading-state overlays or replaces the content within the same centered main content band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main heading sits at the upper-left of the central content band, aligned to the left column of the 12-column grid. It begins roughly 48–64px beneath the bottom edge of the fixed header and is inset from the container’s left edge by the site’s standard content padding (about 24–32px). The heading spans the width of the left column (roughly 30–35% of the total content width, about 4 grid columns), with a generous amount of white space above and to its right, allowing it to stand out without feeling crowded. Vertically, the top of the heading aligns approximately with the top of the first right-column list (specializations), mirroring the way the mockup’s form aligns with the main title, establishing a shared baseline between the two columns.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the main flow; anchors the left column and sets the visual baseline for the top of the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The body text block is positioned directly below the main heading in the same left column, separated from it by a vertical gap of about 16–24px to maintain the mockup’s comfortable rhythm. It is left-aligned and constrained to the same column width as the heading, with a line length around 60–70 characters to keep an editorial, readable feel. Paragraphs are kept short, with 8–16px spacing between them, creating a calm, low-density text area. The right edge of this text block leaves a clear gutter between the left and right columns, allowing the larger vertical block of right-column lists to sit visually beside, not under, the story text. The bottom of the body text stops well above the bottom of the right-column content, reinforcing the sense that the left side introduces the brand while the right side provides extended detail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the left column, stacked directly under the heading; shrinks or grows vertically based on content but maintains consistent top and side margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The specializations list occupies the upper portion of the right column, aligning horizontally with the heading’s baseline on the left. It starts at approximately the same vertical position as the main heading, but on the right-hand 8 columns of the grid (about 65–70% of the content width), inset from the container’s right edge by the same horizontal padding (24–32px). The list spans the full width of this right column. Each specialization appears as a row or subtle card-like item, stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing between items and 16–24px padding inside, echoing the input-like form field proportions from the mockup. A modest section title (if used) would sit above the first item with 8–16px spacing. The overall block forms the top part of a tall vertical column that pulls the eye downward.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the right column, defining the starting point of the right-column vertical stack; its height expands as items are added but maintains consistent spacing to the elements above and below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service highlights list is placed directly beneath the specializations list within the right column, separated by a vertical gap of about 24–32px to signal a distinct but related section. It continues to span the full width of the right column, honoring the same left and right insets. Items in this list are stacked vertically in a similar style—clean, lightly bordered or separated by subtle dividers—maintaining the same 12–16px spacing between items and 16–24px internal padding. This section forms the middle portion of the tall right-column block, creating a continuous vertical rhythm from top to bottom. The visual height of this list is designed to feel substantial but not overwhelming, so that the awards section below still has room to breathe.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the right column, stacked under the specializations list; maintains consistent vertical spacing above and below to preserve the strong downward reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The awards and certifications list sits at the bottom of the right column, directly beneath the service highlights list and separated from it by approximately 24–32px of vertical space. It also spans the full width of the right column with the same horizontal padding and gutter alignment. This section is slightly more compact: items may be shorter rows or badges, still stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing between them. The overall height of this block rounds out the tall right-hand column, ensuring that the right side feels full and informative from top to bottom without appearing dense. The bottom of this list aligns roughly with or slightly below the lowest element in the left column (the navigation links), so the two columns terminate in a visually balanced way.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the right column; its height varies with the number of awards, but it maintains a clear bottom margin (around 48px) before the page’s footer or next section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact navigation link is positioned in the left column beneath the body text, separated from the last paragraph by about 24–32px to distinguish it as an action area. It is left-aligned and may appear as a text-based button or a minimally styled link with an understated underline, matching the editorial tone. Horizontally, it begins flush with the text’s left edge and does not extend beyond the left column width. Vertically, it is typically the first of two links in a small cluster: the Contact link appears first, with the Packages link located to its right with 16–24px horizontal spacing, or stacked below on smaller screens. This placement gives users a clear path to inquire about services without overshadowing the core About content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the left column, directly under the body text; behaves like an inline block that can sit beside the packages-navigation link on wider viewports, stacking vertically on narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The packages/navigation link is placed adjacent to the contact link within the left column, forming a small horizontal pair of actions. On wider screens, it sits to the right of the Contact link with a horizontal gap of about 16–24px, aligned to the same baseline, and constrained within the left column width. On narrower screens, it drops below the Contact link with vertical spacing of about 8–12px. The visual treatment is similar—subtle text-based button or refined link styling—so this link feels like a sibling option rather than a competing primary CTA. The combined links form a compact action cluster that occupies only the lower portion of the left column, leaving plenty of white space below before any next page section or the footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element paired with the contact navigation; aligns horizontally beside it when space allows, otherwise stacks below, maintaining the same left alignment and column constraints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading state indicator appears in place of the main content band whenever data is being fetched. It is centered within the same max-width container beneath the header, respecting the same left and right padding and starting roughly 48–64px below the header. Visually, it may present as a centered spinner accompanied by one or more horizontal skeleton bars that roughly mimic the height and positions of the left heading/body and right-column lists—suggesting a two-column structure without showing real content. The indicator occupies enough vertical space (at least 40–60% of the viewport height) to feel deliberate and to anchor the eye in the center of the screen. Once loading completes, this element disappears entirely, replaced by the full two-column About layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily primary when active: it overlays or replaces the content within the about-content-loader area, centered both horizontally and vertically within the main content band; removed from the flow once data is loaded.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page for viewing current contact details and submitting general inquiries that are not tied to a specific package, quote request, or booking.

core_features: Fetch and display current contact details (phone numbers, email addresses, physical address, business hours, and expected response time) by calling getContactPageContent on page load.
Provide a general contact form bound to submitContactInquiry for users to send non-package, non-quote-related questions.
Pre-fill the contact form subject field from the optional subject URL parameter when present (e.g., when navigated from About or FAQ pages).
Validate required form fields on the client side before calling submitContactInquiry and surface any errors back to the user.
Display a clear success or error message based on the response from submitContactInquiry, including basic status feedback from the ContactInquiry object if available.
Expose direct interaction options (click-to-call, click-to-email) using the dynamic contact details returned by getContactPageContent.
user_workflows:
Workflow A – View contact details only:
1) User navigates to contact.html (with or without URL parameters).
2) On initialization, the page calls getContactPageContent with no parameters.
3) The Contact Info component receives the response and displays the phoneNumbers, emailAddresses, address, businessHours, and expectedResponseTime.
4) User may click on a phone number (initiates tel: link), click on an email address (initiates mailto: link), or read business hours and response time expectations without using the form.

Workflow B – Submit a general inquiry from a direct visit:
1) User navigates to contact.html without a subject URL parameter.
2) Page initialization calls getContactPageContent and populates the Contact Info and Response Time components.
3) Contact Form component initializes with empty fields (name, email, phone, subject, message).
4) User fills in required fields (at minimum: name, email, message; additional required fields can be enforced by implementation rules) and clicks the submit button.
5) The Contact Form component validates the input locally; if valid, it calls submitContactInquiry with { name, email, phone, subject, message }.
6) While submitContactInquiry is in progress, the form shows a submitting state and disables resubmission.
7) On a successful response with success=true, the component reads the returned contactInquiry object for confirmation metadata if needed and displays a success message; the form fields may be cleared or kept read-only according to implementation rules.
8) On a response with success=false or an error, the component displays an error message and allows the user to retry.

Workflow C – Submit an inquiry with a pre-filled subject from About/FAQ:
1) User clicks a "Contact" or similar link on about.html or faq.html that navigates to contact.html with a subject URL parameter (e.g., contact.html?subject=Booking%20Question).
2) The Contact page parses the subject URL parameter on load before or alongside the call to getContactPageContent.
3) The parsed subject value is passed as the initial value to the Contact Form component’s subject field.
4) The user reviews or edits the pre-filled subject and fills in the remaining fields (name, email, phone, message).
5) On form submission, the component calls submitContactInquiry with the potentially edited subject and other field values.
6) The user receives success or error feedback as in Workflow B.

Workflow D – Use contact details to initiate direct communication:
1) User visits contact.html (with or without subject URL parameter) and the page calls getContactPageContent.
2) Contact Info component renders phoneNumbers and emailAddresses as interactive elements.
3) User clicks on a phone number to open the default calling application or device dialer via a tel: link, or clicks an email address, which opens their default email client via a mailto: link with the email address pre-filled.
4) Optionally, the implementation can include the current page URL or subject in the mailto body or subject line as long as it uses only dynamically fetched values and the URL parameter.
interactions:
On page load (init interaction):
- Parse the subject value from the URL, if present (e.g., contact.html?subject=...).
- Trigger getContactPageContent and, on success, populate components that display phoneNumbers, emailAddresses, address, businessHours, and expectedResponseTime.
- Initialize the contact form fields; set the subject input’s initial value from the URL parameter when available, otherwise leave it empty.

On click of a phone number in the contact info:
- User clicks an item representing a phone number from the phoneNumbers array.
- The component triggers navigation using a tel: link constructed from that number.
- No API calls are made; this is a client-side action only.

On click of an email address in the contact info:
- User clicks an item representing an email address from the emailAddresses array.
- The component triggers navigation using a mailto: link constructed from that address.
- No additional API calls are made; this is a client-side action only.

On change of any contact form field (name, email, phone, subject, message):
- The component updates its internal form state with the new value.
- Validation can run on change or on blur (e.g., basic email format, required fields not empty) but does not trigger submitContactInquiry.
- The URL parameter subject is not automatically updated; it is only used as the initial value on load.

On contact form submit:
- User clicks the submit button.
- The component prevents default browser form submission and runs client-side validation.
- If validation fails, the component displays inline error messages and does not call submitContactInquiry.
- If validation passes, the component calls submitContactInquiry with the current form state { name, email, phone, subject, message }.
- The submit button is disabled and a loading indicator is shown until the promise resolves.
- On a successful API response (success=true), the component:
  - May clear the form fields or mark them as readonly.
  - Displays a success message using the response.message and, if needed, data from contactInquiry (e.g., status).
- On an unsuccessful response (success=false) or request error, the component displays an error message and re-enables the submit button for retry.

On API error for getContactPageContent:
- If getContactPageContent fails, an error state is displayed in the Contact Info component (e.g., generic message indicating contact details cannot be loaded right now).
- The Contact Form component remains available so the user can still submit an inquiry, as it does not depend on getContactPageContent data.
- A retry interaction (e.g., a "Retry" button) may re-trigger getContactPageContent.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Contact page state is primarily driven by the subject URL parameter and the responses from getContactPageContent and submitContactInquiry.

URL Parameter Handling:
- The page reads the subject query parameter from the URL on initial load only (e.g., contact.html?subject=General%20Inquiry).
- If subject is present and not empty, its decoded value is used as the initial value for the Contact Form component's subject field.
- If subject is absent or empty, the Contact Form subject field starts empty.
- The page does not write or update URL parameters when the user edits the subject field; the URL parameter is treated as an initial state hint only.

Data Fetch State (getContactPageContent):
- Initial state: { loading: true, error: null, data: null }.
- On successful getContactPageContent response: { loading: false, error: null, data: { phoneNumbers, emailAddresses, address, businessHours, expectedResponseTime } }.
- On failed getContactPageContent response or network error: { loading: false, error: errorObjectOrMessage, data: null }.
- The Contact Info and Response Time components render loading indicators or error messages based on this state.

Form State (submitContactInquiry):
- Form state includes fields: name, email, phone, subject, message, and metadata such as validationErrors, isSubmitting, submitError, submitSuccess.
- On submit, isSubmitting switches to true; submitError and submitSuccess are reset.
- On successful submitContactInquiry response (success=true):
  - isSubmitting is set to false.
  - submitSuccess is set to true.
  - The returned contactInquiry object is stored in component state for possible display (e.g., showing its status field), although it is not persisted via URL.
- On unsuccessful response (success=false) or request error:
  - isSubmitting is set to false.
  - submitError is set to the returned or derived error message.
  - submitSuccess remains false.

No localStorage or sessionStorage is used. All persistent/meaningful state relevant to routing is derived from the URL parameters and the live API responses.

components.functionality: Controls initial page behavior: parses URL parameters, triggers loading of contact details, and passes the initial subject value to the Contact Form component.

Fetches and displays contact details including phone numbers, email addresses, physical address, and business hours using getContactPageContent. Also provides click-to-call and click-to-email interactions.
Displays the expected response time for inquiries based on the expectedResponseTime value returned by getContactPageContent.
Handles user input for general contact inquiries (name, email, phone, subject, message), performs client-side validation, and submits the inquiry via submitContactInquiry.
Shows real-time status messages related to the contact form submission, including loading state, success feedback, and error messages based on the submitContactInquiry response.
Manages and displays loading and error states for the initial fetch of contact details via getContactPageContent, coordinating with the contact-info-panel and response-time-note components.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a constrained max-width content area centered in the viewport. The left text block occupies roughly 4 columns and the right form block around 8 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: A full-width, fixed-height header with centered navigation sits at the very top (shared across the site). The Contact page components live in the main content band beneath it. Within that band, content is arranged in a two-column layout on a white background: the left column holds the contact-info-panel at the top followed immediately by the response-time-note, while the right column contains the contact-form with the contact-form-status directly above its fields. The contact-content-loader and contact-page-init are structural, non-visual controllers that overlay or manage this main band without introducing new visible blocks.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: A full-width, fixed-height header with centered navigation sits at the very top (shared across the site). The Contact page components live in the main content band beneath it. Within that band, content is arranged in a two-column layout on a white background: the left column holds the contact-info-panel at the top followed immediately by the response-time-note, while the right column contains the contact-form with the contact-form-status directly above its fields. The contact-content-loader and contact-page-init are structural, non-visual controllers that overlay or manage this main band without introducing new visible blocks.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport is reserved for the full-width header. Below that, a generous vertical gap (~56–64px) introduces the main content band. Inside this band, the layout follows an implicit 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container: the left column (contact-info-panel + response-time-note) occupies about 4/12 of the width (~35%), and the right column (contact-form + contact-form-status) occupies about 8/12 (~65%), emphasizing the form as the primary interactive area. Vertical spacing between stacked elements (info vs. note, status vs. form fields) stays within the 16–32px range to maintain the mockup’s spacious rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: The left column is low-density: a concise block of contact details with short line lengths and one small supporting note, surrounded by ample white space. The right column is moderately dense: form fields are stacked vertically with consistent spacing, using full column width but never feeling cramped thanks to 8–12px internal input padding and 16–24px gaps between fields. Overall, there is more negative space than content, keeping the page calm and uncluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first encounters the colored header and centered navigation; the active state on the Contact/Join-style nav item anchors them to this page. From there, the eye drops down to the top of the main content band, starting in the left column with the primary contact information heading and key details. The gaze then naturally shifts right to the dominant contact form block, beginning at any form title/status area and then moving straight down the vertical column of fields. If a loader or error state is active, the contact-content-loader temporarily becomes the focal point, centered over both columns while preserving the underlying structure.

layout.overall_description: Beneath a fixed, full-width header bar with centered navigation, the Contact page content sits in a broad, white main band centered in the viewport. This band is constrained to a max-width and divided into two columns on a 12-column grid. On the left, a relatively narrow column holds the core contact information and a short response-time note in a tidy vertical stack, with plenty of white space around and below. On the right, a wider column presents the main contact form as a tall, continuous block: a subtle status/feedback strip appears at the top when needed, followed by full-width form fields arranged in a clean vertical rhythm. The entire section feels airy and editorial, with generous spacing between header, columns, and individual elements.
