When loading or errors occur, a centered overlay from the contact-content-loader gently dims or replaces the main band, drawing attention without breaking the underlying two-column composition.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lifecycle controller is attached at the root of the Contact page’s main content area, logically wrapping the entire two-column contact section that sits below the global header. It does not render any visible UI: it occupies no width or height and introduces no margins or padding. Visually, the layout appears unchanged whether this controller is present or not; all visible alignment is governed by the main content container that centers the two-column layout beneath the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-intrusive; it lives at the top of the main content DOM hierarchy, managing state without affecting the document flow or the spatial relationships of other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-panel sits in the left column of the main content band, directly beneath the header’s lower edge. The main content band begins approximately 56–64px below the header, and the top of the contact-info-panel aligns horizontally with the top edge of the contact-form on the right, creating a shared baseline. Within the centered max-width container, this panel occupies roughly 4 of 12 grid columns (about 35% of the content width), starting flush with the container’s left inner padding. It presents the primary contact heading and key details in a compact vertical stack, with short line lengths and generous whitespace to its right, above, and below. The right edge of this panel aligns with the visual gutter between the two columns, leaving a clear, consistent vertical boundary before the right-side form begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element anchored to the left side of the two-column grid; it scales responsively with the container but maintains its approximate 4/12 width until smaller breakpoints where the layout may naturally stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The response-time-note is placed directly below the contact-info-panel in the same left column, forming a simple top-to-bottom stack. It spans the same effective width as the contact-info-panel (the full 4-column left segment), with its left edge aligned exactly with the panel above. A vertical gap of roughly 16–24px separates it from the bottom of the contact-info-panel, small enough to feel connected but large enough to read as a distinct line of supporting information. The text is set in a slightly smaller, lighter style, so visually it reads as a subtle footnote under the main contact details while still comfortably surrounded by whitespace at the bottom of the column before the page’s lower margin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline/paragraph-level block within the left column’s flow; it does not introduce new columns or overlays and simply extends the left column’s vertical content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form occupies the entire right column of the main content band, emphasizing it as the primary interactive area. It begins at the same vertical position as the top of the contact-info-panel, with its left edge aligned to the grid gutter that separates it from the left column. This right column spans approximately 8 of 12 grid columns (about 60–65% of the content width), and the form’s content stretches nearly the full width of this column, leaving only modest side padding for comfort. If the form includes its own heading, that heading sits at the top of this block, directly above the first field (or above the contact-form-status when visible). The input fields are arranged in a single, vertical stack: each field spans the full column width, with 16–24px of vertical space between field groups, and each input uses around 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal internal padding. The bottom of the form naturally extends further down the page than the left column text, reinforcing the strong vertical reading path on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-height block within the right column; it grows vertically with the number of fields and maintains its relative width on the 12-column grid. On smaller screens it can collapse below the left column, but in the primary layout it stays right-aligned, forming the dominant vertical block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form-status appears as a horizontal feedback banner positioned at the very top of the contact-form block, inside the right column. When present, it spans the same width as the form fields, with its left and right edges aligned to the form’s inner padding, giving the impression of a full-width strip above the fields. It sits approximately 8–16px above the first input field, creating a tight but clear separation that visually binds the status message to the form it describes. The banner’s height is modest—typically one or two text lines with 8–12px padding above and below the text—so it feels like a compact, inline notification rather than a heavy panel. When no status is active, this component collapses completely, freeing the space so the form fields shift up to occupy the top of the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the contact-form’s vertical flow; it does not overlay content but instead pushes form fields downward when visible, preserving the clean vertical rhythm of the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-content-loader controls the loading and error presentation for the entire contact section. Visually, when active, it appears centered within the main content band that contains both columns. In loading states, it presents a minimalist indicator (spinner or progress hint) positioned at the geometric center of the combined left-right content area, while a subtle, semi-transparent overlay dims the contact-info-panel and contact-form underneath without changing their positions. This preserves the perception of the two-column layout while clearly signaling that the content is temporarily inactive. In error states, the loader can present an inline message in that same central position, often spanning a width similar to the right column, with enough padding (16–24px) and margin from the top of the band (~48–64px) to feel deliberate and spacious. The overlay ensures that the user’s eye is immediately drawn to the loader content, temporarily making it the focal point of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an overlay anchored to the main contact content container: positioned absolutely or relatively within that container so it covers both the left and right columns while active. When inactive, it is effectively invisible and occupies no visual space, allowing the standard two-column layout to remain fully interactive.

name: FAQ
title: FAQ
description: Page for viewing and filtering frequently asked questions about catering services, using category filters synced with the URL and dynamic FAQ data from the backend.

core_features: Load and display FAQ entries from the backend using the getFAQItems interface, optionally filtered by the category URL parameter.
Allow users to change the FAQ category filter (e.g., ordering, pricing, dietary, booking) and update both the displayed FAQs and the URL parameter.
Provide expandable FAQ items (accordion-style) so users can quickly scan questions and reveal answers on demand.
Offer an optional client-side keyword filter over the currently loaded FAQ items without making additional API calls.
Provide a clear path to contact.html with a prefilled subject for users whose questions are not answered by the FAQ.
user_workflows:
View general FAQ list:
1. User navigates to faq.html without URL parameters.
2. The page state manager reads the URL, finds no category parameter, and treats the category filter as unset.
3. The page calls getFAQItems with no category argument.
4. The FAQ list component renders all returned FAQItem records (subject to any client-side keyword filter, if used).
5. The user expands/collapses individual FAQ items to read answers.

View FAQs for a specific category via deep link:
1. User clicks a link from another page (e.g., index.html or contact.html) that points to faq.html?category={value}.
2. On load, the page state manager reads the category parameter from the URL.
3. The page calls getFAQItems with that category value.
4. The category filter component marks the corresponding category as active based on the URL.
5. The FAQ list component displays only FAQItem records returned for that category.
6. If the user clears the category filter, the category parameter is removed from the URL and getFAQItems is called again without a category argument.

Change category from within the FAQ page:
1. User arrives on faq.html (with or without an existing category parameter).
2. User selects a different category from the category filter controls.
3. The filter component updates the URL category parameter using pushState/replaceState (without reloading the page).
4. The page state manager detects this change and calls getFAQItems with the new category value.
5. The FAQ list updates to show the corresponding set of FAQItem records.

Use keyword filtering within the loaded FAQ items:
1. User is viewing a set of FAQ items already loaded from getFAQItems (with or without a category filter).
2. User types a keyword into the keyword filter input.
3. The FAQ list component applies a client-side filter to the already-loaded FAQItem array, matching the keyword against question and/or answer text.
4. The displayed FAQ items update in real time to reflect the keyword filter.
5. Clearing the keyword input removes the client-side filter and restores the full set from the current getFAQItems result.

Navigate to contact for unanswered questions:
1. User reads through relevant FAQ items but still needs clarification.
2. User clicks the "Still have questions? Contact us" action.
3. The page navigates to contact.html with the query parameter subject=Question%20about%20FAQ.
4. On contact.html, the subject field can be prefilled from the subject parameter (handled by that page).
interactions:
Page load and initial data fetch:
- Trigger: Browser requests faq.html (with or without ?category=...).
- Behavior: The page state manager reads the category URL parameter. It then calls getFAQItems with the category value if present, or with no argument if not. The returned array of FAQItem records is stored in page state and passed to the FAQ list component.

Category change via filter controls:
- Trigger: User clicks or selects a category option in the category filter component.
- Behavior: The filter component emits a category change event with the selected category value (or an empty value to clear the filter). The state manager updates the URL category parameter accordingly and re-invokes getFAQItems with the new category value (or with no argument if cleared). The FAQ list re-renders with the new data.

URL-driven category state (back/forward navigation):
- Trigger: User uses the browser back or forward buttons, changing the URL category parameter.
- Behavior: The state manager listens to popstate events, reads the updated category parameter, calls getFAQItems with that value, and updates the active category in the filter component and the FAQ list contents.

Expand/collapse an FAQ item:
- Trigger: User clicks on a question row or an expand/collapse control within a FAQ item.
- Behavior: The FAQ item accordion component toggles an internal expanded/collapsed state for that item. The answer content is shown or hidden accordingly. This interaction does not call any API and does not modify the URL.

Apply keyword filter:
- Trigger: User types into or clears the keyword filter input.
- Behavior: The FAQ list component applies client-side filtering over the currently loaded FAQItem array, based on the keyword; it does not call getFAQItems again. Only items whose question and/or answer contain the keyword (according to implementation) remain visible. Clearing the input removes the keyword filter.

Contact link click:
- Trigger: User clicks the "Still have questions? Contact us" link/button.
- Behavior: The page navigates the user to contact.html?subject=Question%20about%20FAQ. No API calls are made from the FAQ page for this interaction.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The FAQ page state is primarily driven by the 'category' URL parameter and the results of the getFAQItems call.

- URL parameter handling:
  - On initial load, the page reads window.location.search to obtain the category parameter value (if present).
  - If a non-empty category value is present, the page passes that value to getFAQItems(category). If the category parameter is absent or empty, getFAQItems is called without arguments.
  - The current category value is treated as the source of truth for the active category filter; both the filter UI and the FAQ list respect it.

- Data loading:
  - getFAQItems returns an array of FAQItem records. The page keeps this array in memory as the "currentFAQItems" state.
  - Any time the category parameter changes (via filter interaction or browser navigation), the page re-calls getFAQItems with the new category context and replaces currentFAQItems with the new result.

- Client-side filters:
  - Keyword filtering is applied purely client-side on currentFAQItems. The keyword itself is tracked in component-level state but is not synchronized to the URL and does not affect getFAQItems calls.

- URL updates on user actions:
  - When the user selects a category via the filter component, the page updates the URL query string: if a category is selected, 'category' is set to that value; if the category is cleared, the 'category' parameter is removed from the URL.
  - History API (pushState or replaceState) is used so that browser navigation correctly restores prior category selections.

- Non-persistent UI state:
  - Accordion expanded/collapsed states are held in in-memory component state keyed by FAQItem.id and are not represented in the URL.
  - No use of localStorage or other persistent client storage is involved; only URL parameters and in-memory state control behavior.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and subsequent updates based on the category URL parameter; orchestrates calls to getFAQItems and distributes the resulting FAQItem array and active category value to child components.

Provides controls for selecting or clearing a FAQ category filter. Reflects the active category derived from the URL and emits events when the user changes the selection. The available category options can be derived from the FAQItem.category values returned by getFAQItems or from the known category enumeration, without hardcoding any FAQ content.
Accepts a user-entered keyword and applies a client-side text filter over the currently loaded FAQItem array, without invoking getFAQItems. It does not modify the URL parameters.

Receives the currentFAQItems array (already filtered by category via getFAQItems and optionally by keyword via the keyword filter) and renders a list of FAQ items. Delegates per-item expand/collapse behavior to faq-item-accordion components.

Represents a single FAQ entry. Displays the FAQItem.question and, when expanded, the FAQItem.answer. Manages its own expanded/collapsed state keyed by FAQItem.id and notifies the parent list component when toggled.
Provides a clear call-to-action for users who still have questions. On activation, navigates to contact.html with the subject query parameter set to indicate that the inquiry relates to the FAQ page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a constrained max-width content area centered in the viewport. The left text block occupies roughly 4 columns and the right form block around 8 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses the same overall pattern as the mockup: a full-width fixed header (global) with centered navigation, followed by a main content band on a white background. Inside the main band, content is arranged in a two-column layout within a centered, max-width container on a 12-column grid. The left column (about 4/12 width) is reserved for the FAQ page heading and explanatory copy (provided outside these components), while the right column (about 8/12 width) hosts the interactive FAQ experience: keyword search at the top, category filters just below it, then a vertically stacked FAQ list of accordion items, ending with a contact CTA. The top of the right column content aligns vertically with the left column headline, echoing the headline–form alignment from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses the same overall pattern as the mockup: a full-width fixed header (global) with centered navigation, followed by a main content band on a white background. Inside the main band, content is arranged in a two-column layout within a centered, max-width container on a 12-column grid. The left column (about 4/12 width) is reserved for the FAQ page heading and explanatory copy (provided outside these components), while the right column (about 8/12 width) hosts the interactive FAQ experience: keyword search at the top, category filters just below it, then a vertically stacked FAQ list of accordion items, ending with a contact CTA. The top of the right column content aligns vertically with the left column headline, echoing the headline–form alignment from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Approximately the top 15–20% of the viewport is occupied by the fixed header bar. Below that, a generous vertical gap of roughly 48–64px separates the header from the main content band. The main content container spans about 70–80% of the viewport width (max ~1100–1200px) and is horizontally centered. Inside this container, the left column occupies roughly one-third of the width and the right column roughly two-thirds, visually emphasizing the interactive FAQ tools and list. Within the right column, the keyword filter, category filter, FAQ list, and CTA follow a clear top-to-bottom sequence, each separated by 16–32px vertical spacing to maintain rhythm and clarity.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density mirrors the moderately spacious feel of the mockup. The left column contains minimal text with short, readable lines. The right column is denser but still airy: the keyword input and filters use comfortable padding (around 8–12px vertical, 12–16px horizontal inside fields), with 16–24px gaps between stacked elements. FAQ accordion items are full-width but separated by consistent vertical spacing, avoiding visual clutter even with many questions. Overall, the page feels calm and organized, suitable for an upscale service site.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first catches the colored fixed header and centered navigation, with an active state indicating the FAQ section. From there, attention drops into the main content, landing on the large 'FAQ' or 'Frequently Asked Questions' headline in the left column and its short descriptive paragraph. Because the right column is visually heavier and taller, the eye naturally moves next to the topmost interactive control there—the keyword filter—then down to the category filters beneath it. From the filters, the eye continues straight down the strong vertical stack of accordion questions in the FAQ list. At the end of this list, a clearly differentiated FAQ contact CTA card acts as the closing anchor, guiding users to reach out if their question isn’t answered.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed, full-width header with centered navigation, the FAQ page reveals a wide, white content band centered on the screen, constrained to a refined max-width. The main area is split into two columns: on the left, occupying about one-third of the width, sits the FAQ page title and a short, calm introduction about the catering services and what the FAQ covers. On the right, taking up the remaining two-thirds, a vertical stack of interactive elements echoes the form-like column from the mockup: at the top, a full-width keyword search input aligns horizontally with the page title; just below, a row or block of category filter controls allows users to narrow questions by theme. Beneath these, a tall, vertically flowing list of FAQ accordion items fills most of the column, each question presented as a clean, full-width row that can expand to reveal the answer. Generous whitespace between rows prevents crowding while maintaining a strong, continuous vertical reading path. At the bottom of the right column, slightly inset and framed with a subtle background, a contact CTA panel invites users to get in touch if they still need help, acting as the visual and functional endpoint of the page. All elements scroll together under the fixed header, preserving a quiet, editorial feel with consistent spacing drawn from the 8px-based system.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ page state manager sits conceptually at the top of the main content area, just inside the page’s central container and above all visual FAQ components in the document structure. It spans the full available width of the content container but occupies no visible space—no background, border, or padding—so it does not affect the visual spacing. Its role is to orchestrate state for the keyword and category filters and the FAQ list, ensuring that interactions (like changing a category or updating the URL to reflect filter state) propagate downward. All visible FAQ-related components (keyword filter, category filter, FAQ list, and contact CTA) are nested beneath it, both logically and structurally, but it remains invisible in the rendered layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logical wrapper; occupies no height or width visually and scrolls with the page as part of the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category filter is positioned in the right column of the main content, directly below the keyword filter and above the FAQ list. Within the approximately 8/12-width right column, it spans nearly the full width (about 90–100% of the column), aligned flush to the left with the keyword search input above to create a clean vertical edge. Vertically, there is a gap of roughly 16–24px between the bottom of the keyword filter and the top of the category filter, matching the form-field spacing in the mockup. The filter controls themselves (e.g., pill-shaped buttons or tabs) are arranged horizontally left-to-right with about 16–24px horizontal spacing between items, and 8–12px vertical padding inside each pill, maintaining a moderately spacious feel. If the number of categories is large, they wrap onto a second line or allow horizontal scrolling, but the overall block still feels like a cohesive band. The block’s top aligns vertically with the first row of FAQ items below, reinforcing a tidy, stacked rhythm in the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the standard scrollable right column content; it maintains its relative position beneath the keyword filter and above the FAQ list as the user scrolls. No fixed or sticky behavior by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The keyword filter is the first interactive element in the right column and acts as the visual counterpart to the form’s top field in the mockup. It is aligned so that the top edge of its input field matches the vertical baseline of the left-column FAQ page heading text, creating a clear horizontal alignment across columns. Within the main container, it sits in the right 8/12 of the grid, occupying nearly the full width of that column (around 90–100%), with its left edge aligned to the left edge of the category filter and FAQ list below. The input has generous internal padding (about 8–12px vertically and 12–16px horizontally), a subtle border or outline, and a muted placeholder, preserving the minimalist, upscale aesthetic. There is a vertical gap of roughly 48–64px between the bottom of the fixed header and the top of the main content band; within that band, a further 16–24px separates the top of the right column (keyword filter) from any internal section label if present. Overall, the keyword filter sets the top of the right column’s vertical rhythm, with all subsequent elements stacking neatly beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, scrolling naturally with the page under the fixed header. It does not remain pinned as the user scrolls; its prominence comes from its position at the top of the interactive stack and its full-width presentation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ list occupies the majority of the right column’s vertical space, creating the tall columnar block that visually echoes the stacked form fields from the mockup. It is positioned directly beneath the category filter, with a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px separating the bottom of the filter from the top of the first FAQ item. The list spans the full width of the right column (about 8/12 of the page width within the max-width container), aligned to the same left and right boundaries as the keyword and category filters above. FAQs are rendered as a vertical sequence of accordion items, each full-width within the list. Between each FAQ item, there is a consistent vertical spacing of about 16–24px to maintain a calm, readable rhythm even as the list grows long. The background of the list area remains the same white as the main content band, so the structure is defined by the accordions themselves and the spacing rather than by a separate container box. As users scroll, the list extends downward, while the left column content typically remains shorter, reinforcing the right column as the main interaction area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides within the scrollable main content area, growing vertically as more FAQ items are present. It scrolls under the fixed header and maintains its relative position beneath the filters and above the contact CTA within the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each FAQ item accordion is a full-width row within the FAQ list, visually akin to a form field block from the mockup. The accordion’s closed state consists of a question title line stretching from left to right within the right column, with the question text left-aligned and a subtle expand/collapse icon or chevron aligned to the far right. The row has comfortable internal padding—around 12–16px horizontally and 8–12px vertically—and may be outlined by a thin, muted border or a very light bottom divider to keep the layout minimalist. When expanded, the answer content appears directly below the question within the same block, indented slightly from the left edge or simply separated by 8–12px of vertical padding. The answer text uses shorter line lengths corresponding to the right column width, maintaining readability. A small gap of 16–24px is maintained between the bottom of one accordion item and the top of the next, giving each item room to breathe. Across all items, alignment of titles, icons, and padding is consistent, reinforcing the strong vertical rhythm seen in the mockup’s form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained inside the FAQ list; each accordion expands and collapses in place without shifting its horizontal position. As items expand, they push subsequent items downward, preserving the linear vertical flow. All accordion items scroll with the list in the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ contact CTA sits at the bottom of the right column, directly following the last FAQ item in the list, analogous to the submit area at the end of a form. There is a generous vertical gap of roughly 32–48px between the bottom of the final accordion item and the top of this CTA, giving it a clear separation as a concluding element. The CTA occupies most of the width of the right column (approximately 70–90% of the column), with its left edge aligned to the left edge of the FAQ list content, and a slightly shorter width than the list can subtly frame it as a card. It may use a gently tinted background or fine border to distinguish it from the white page background while remaining understated and consistent with the muted palette. Inside the CTA, a concise heading like “Still have questions?” appears at the top, followed by a short line or two of text and a primary action button (e.g., “Contact Us” or “Request a Call”), all aligned left and stacked with 8–16px of vertical spacing. This block forms the final visual anchor of the vertical flow on the page, inviting users to transition from self-service FAQ browsing to direct contact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scroll behavior within the right column; it remains at the bottom of the FAQ content and comes into view as the user reaches the end of the list. It does not stick or float but serves as a natural stopping point within the page’s linear flow.

name: Policies
title: Policies
description: Page for viewing all service and website policies, including privacy, terms of service, and booking-related policies, with internal navigation and deep-linking via URL parameters.

core_features: Load and display a list of all active policy pages using listPolicyPages, including their titles and summaries for quick scanning and navigation.
Allow users to focus on a specific policy by selecting it from the list or via URL parameters (policyId or type), loading full content through getPolicyPage.
Provide internal navigation within the Policies page so users can switch between different policies without leaving the page.
Support deep-linking from other pages (e.g., cart, home) using policyId or type URL parameters so users land directly on the relevant policy.
Highlight the currently active policy in the list and synchronize it with the URL parameters so the current view can be bookmarked or shared.
Display policy metadata such as lastUpdated from the PolicyPage model to inform users about the recency of each policy.
user_workflows:
View all policies
1. User navigates to policies.html (no URL parameters).
2. The page-init component calls listPolicyPages to retrieve all active policies and their summaries.
3. The sidebar/list component displays all policies as clickable items.
4. The page selects a default policy (e.g., the first active policy in the returned list) and calls getPolicyPage with the corresponding policyId.
5. The full content of the selected policy is shown in the content viewer, and the selected policy is highlighted in the list.

Open a specific policy from another page via type
1. User clicks a "Policies"-related link on another page (e.g., cart.html) that directs to policies.html?type={policyType}.
2. On load, the page-init component reads the type URL parameter.
3. listPolicyPages is called to retrieve all policies; the page attempts to find a policy whose policy.type matches the type URL parameter.
4. If a matching policy is found, getPolicyPage is called with type set to the URL parameter; the returned PolicyPage becomes the active policy.
5. The sidebar/list highlights the matching policy; the content viewer displays its full content.
6. If no matching policy is found, the page falls back to the default policy selection and updates the URL to reflect the fallback policyId.

Open a specific policy from another page via policyId
1. User clicks a link like policies.html?policyId={id} from index.html or cart.html.
2. On load, the page-init component reads the policyId URL parameter.
3. listPolicyPages is called to populate the list and verify whether any policy has policy.id equal to the policyId parameter.
4. If a match exists, getPolicyPage is called with policyId set to the URL parameter; the returned PolicyPage becomes the active policy.
5. The sidebar/list highlights that policy, and the content viewer displays its content.
6. If no such policy exists or is inactive, the page selects a default policy and updates the URL accordingly.

Switch between policies using the in-page list
1. User is on policies.html with a policy already displayed.
2. User clicks another policy entry in the sidebar/list.
3. The click handler reads the clicked policy's id and type from the bound data.
4. The URL is updated (e.g., policies.html?policyId={id}) via history API without a full page reload.
5. The page calls getPolicyPage with policyId equal to the clicked policy's id.
6. The active policy in the sidebar/list is updated, and the content viewer re-renders with the new policy content.

Share or bookmark a specific policy
1. User is viewing a policy and wants to share or bookmark it.
2. The content viewer or a dedicated control exposes the current URL (including policyId or type).
3. User copies the URL using their browser or a "Copy link" interaction.
4. When this URL is opened later, the corresponding policy is loaded again using the same URL-parameter-based logic as above.

Handle browser navigation (back/forward) between policies
1. User navigates between policies using the sidebar/list, which updates the URL on each selection.
2. User presses the browser Back button.
3. The url-state-sync component listens to popstate events, reads the new URL parameters, and updates the active policyId and/or type in page state.
4. getPolicyPage is called with the updated parameters, and the content viewer updates accordingly.
5. The sidebar/list updates the highlighted policy to match the restored state.
interactions: Click on a policy in the sidebar/list to select it, triggering a URL update and a getPolicyPage call for that policy.
Automatic selection of a default policy on initial load when no policyId or type parameters are provided, triggering a getPolicyPage call for that default.
Automatic resolution of a policy from the type URL parameter on initial load, calling getPolicyPage with type when a matching policy exists.
Automatic resolution of a policy from the policyId URL parameter on initial load, calling getPolicyPage with policyId when a matching policy exists.
URL synchronization when a user changes the active policy, ensuring policyId (and optionally type) are reflected in the query string.
Handling of browser back/forward events to re-load the appropriate policy based on updated URL parameters via getPolicyPage.
Optional interaction to copy or expose a shareable link to the current policy, using the current URL with its query parameters.
Scroll behavior within the policy content area (client-side) so users can read long policies; no additional interface calls are needed for scrolling.
Optional internal navigation within a long policy using anchors derived from the dynamic content string, driven entirely on the client side without additional interface calls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven entirely by URL query parameters and data returned from listPolicyPages and getPolicyPage, without using localStorage or other persistent client-side storage. On initial load, the page reads the policyId and type parameters (if present) from the URL. It calls listPolicyPages once to obtain all available policies and uses that list to: (1) render the sidebar/list; (2) validate and resolve the active policy against the URL parameters. Resolution priority is: if policyId is present and matches an active PolicyPage.id from the list, the page calls getPolicyPage(policyId={policyId}). If policyId is missing or invalid but type is present and matches a PolicyPage.type from the list, the page calls getPolicyPage(type={type}). If neither parameter yields a valid policy, the page selects a default policy from the list (e.g., first active entry), sets it as active in the in-memory state, and updates the URL to include its policyId using history.replaceState. Whenever the user selects a policy from the sidebar/list, the page updates the active policyId in state and uses history.pushState to update the URL's policyId parameter, then calls getPolicyPage(policyId={selectedId}). The type parameter may be preserved or updated based on the selected policy's type. Browser back/forward navigation is handled by listening to popstate; when the URL changes, the page re-runs the same resolution logic (policyId first, then type, then fallback) to determine the active policy and calls getPolicyPage accordingly. The rendered content viewer and the highlighted item in the sidebar are always derived from the currently active PolicyPage returned by getPolicyPage. No static or hardcoded policy content is used; all titles, content, and lastUpdated values are taken from the PolicyPage entities returned by the interfaces.

components.functionality: Initializes the Policies page by reading URL parameters, loading the list of policies, resolving the active policy, and fetching its full content.
Displays all policies returned by listPolicyPages as a clickable list or table of contents, showing each policy's title and summary and highlighting the currently active policy.
Renders the full content for the currently active PolicyPage, including the title, content string, and lastUpdated metadata; supports scrolling and optional internal anchors derived from the content.
Keeps the URL query parameters (policyId and type) synchronized with the currently active policy and responds to browser navigation changes by updating the active policy.
Provides a way for users to copy or view a shareable URL for the currently active policy, based on the current policyId or type present in the URL.
Displays metadata for the active policy, such as its lastUpdated date, using the PolicyPage fields to inform users about the policy's recency.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a constrained max-width content area centered in the viewport. The left text block occupies roughly 4 columns and the right form block around 8 columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top‑to‑bottom flow: a full‑width fixed header/navigation at the very top (inherited from the site’s global layout). Beneath this, a single, spacious white content band sits centered within the viewport. Inside this band, content is organized into a two‑column layout on a 12‑column grid: a left column for the policy list (navigation) and a right column for the active policy content. The right column includes a metadata banner at the top and the main policy body below it, with a compact share‑link control aligned in the header area of the policy content. Nonvisual logic components (page-init and URL state sync) operate behind the scenes and do not occupy visible layout space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top‑to‑bottom flow: a full‑width fixed header/navigation at the very top (inherited from the site’s global layout). Beneath this, a single, spacious white content band sits centered within the viewport. Inside this band, content is organized into a two‑column layout on a 12‑column grid: a left column for the policy list (navigation) and a right column for the active policy content. The right column includes a metadata banner at the top and the main policy body below it, with a compact share‑link control aligned in the header area of the policy content. Nonvisual logic components (page-init and URL state sync) operate behind the scenes and do not occupy visible layout space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header consumes roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height and spans full width. The main content band begins after a generous vertical gap of about 56–64px from the bottom of the header, maintaining the airy feel of the mockup. The content band is constrained to a centered max‑width (approximately 1120–1200px) using a 12‑column grid. The left policy list sidebar occupies about 4 of 12 columns (~30–35% width), while the right policy content viewer takes the remaining 8 of 12 columns (~65–70% width), mirroring the mockup’s left text vs. right form emphasis. Vertical spacing between major blocks (policy list header vs. list, metadata banner vs. body content, etc.) is in the 24–48px range, derived from the 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density is moderately spacious. The left policy list sidebar uses compact yet breathable spacing: list items are stacked vertically with 8–12px vertical gaps and comfortable line height so it feels scannable but not cramped. The right policy content viewer has the higher textual density but still respects the editorial aesthetic: generous line height, short to medium line lengths, and 16–24px spacing between paragraphs and subsections. The metadata banner at the top of the content viewer has clear padding (16–24px) and minimal elements, making it feel like a lightweight header rather than a heavy card. The share‑link control is compact and unobtrusive, integrated into the metadata area without adding clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first encounters the colored header bar and central navigation, then naturally follows the underline or active state on the “Policies” nav item. From there, attention drops into the main content band. Within that band, the eye is drawn first to the left column near its top—where the policy list sidebar acts as the structural anchor akin to the ‘Join Our Team’ heading in the mockup. After identifying the relevant policy in the left list, the user’s focus shifts right to the larger policy content viewer. Inside the right column, the gaze starts at the metadata banner (policy title, category, last updated) and the share‑link control near the top‑right, then moves downward through the policy body in a strong vertical reading path, section by section. The sticky behavior of the sidebar (and optionally the metadata banner) supports this vertical flow without sacrificing context.

layout.overall_description: The Policies page sits beneath a full‑width, fixed site header with centered navigation, echoing the mockup’s calm, editorial tone. Below the header, a generous strip of white space leads into a wide but constrained content band centered on the page, framed by muted background tones. Within this band, a two‑column structure appears: on the left, a narrow, vertical sidebar lists all policies in a clean stacked layout; on the right, a broader column presents the currently selected policy in a refined reading view. The left sidebar feels like a stable spine, starting slightly below the header and extending downward, while the right column feels like a spacious article area. At the top of the right column, a subtle metadata banner spans its width, displaying the policy title and key details with calm typography, and a small share‑link control aligned to the top‑right. Below this, the policy text flows vertically with comfortable spacing, mimicking the steady rhythm of form fields in the original mockup. Invisible logic components manage initial page state and deep‑linking without adding visual noise. The result is a balanced, modern layout where navigation and content are clearly separated yet visually cohesive, supporting focused reading and easy movement between policies.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is nonvisual and sits at the page level, conceptually wrapping the entire main content area beneath the global header. It does not render any visible UI or consume spatial dimensions in the layout. Instead, it initializes which policy should be active when the user lands on the page (via URL parameters or default states) before the visual components are drawn. Think of it as an invisible layer spanning the full width and height of the page, operating behind the scenes to set up the initial state for the left sidebar and right content viewer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering; occupies no visual space and does not affect layout flow. It runs on page load and during navigation events to prepare data and state for visual components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy list sidebar aligns to the left edge of the central content band, mirroring the role and approximate width of the left text column in the mockup. Within the 12‑column grid, it spans about 4 columns (~30–35% of the content band’s width). Its top edge begins slightly below the bottom of the global header—separated by a vertical gap of roughly 56–64px—and aligns vertically with the top of the policy metadata banner in the right column for a clean horizontal baseline. The sidebar extends downward, occupying the full height needed for the list, and in longer pages it can scroll along with the content. The list items are arranged in a single vertical stack with each policy title left‑aligned, using short labels and a line length similar to the mockup’s left text block. Between items, allow about 8–12px of vertical spacing so the list feels structured but not sparse. The active or selected policy is visually differentiated (e.g., bolder text and a subtle left border or muted highlight), strengthening the connection to the content on the right. On wider screens, the sidebar may use a subtle sticky behavior: once the user scrolls past the initial header, the sidebar remains anchored near the top of the viewport with a top offset of about 80–96px so that navigation remains accessible while the right column scrolls. On narrower screens, it should collapse into a top section above the content viewer, but still keep a generous margin beneath it to preserve the airy, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the main content band, it is left‑aligned and participates in the normal document flow. On desktop and large tablets, it adopts a sticky position within its column, remaining visible as the user scrolls, with a consistent top offset below the fixed header. On small screens, it stacks above the policy content viewer as a full‑width block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy content viewer occupies the right portion of the central content band and is the dominant visual region, analogous to the form column in the mockup. It starts on the same horizontal baseline as the top of the policy list sidebar, but its width is larger—spanning approximately 8 of the 12 grid columns (~65–70% of the content band). There is a modest horizontal gap of about 24–32px between the sidebar and this viewer, ensuring visual separation while keeping them visually connected. Within this right column, the viewer stretches down the page and is the primary scrollable reading area on larger screens. At its top, it contains the policy metadata banner; directly beneath that, the main policy text flows downward in a single column with a comfortable line length (roughly 60–80 characters) to maintain readability. Paragraphs, headings, and lists are spaced with a consistent vertical rhythm, using 16–24px between paragraphs and 24–32px above section headings, echoing the steady progression of form fields in the source mockup. The background of the viewer remains white or very lightly tinted to match the overall content band, with subtle section dividers (like thin muted lines or spacing) rather than heavy boxes. The overall impression is that of a clean, editorial article, clearly aligned in a strong vertical column that visually outweighs the left sidebar while still feeling part of the same structured grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document layout as the right column of the two‑column grid. It scrolls vertically to reveal longer policies, while the header and (optionally) the sidebar can remain fixed or sticky above. On smaller screens, it stacks below the policy list, adopting full‑width behavior with preserved internal spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is entirely nonvisual and acts as a behavioral layer connecting the selected policy in the sidebar with the URL and the content displayed in the viewer. It has no box, no dimensions, and does not appear on screen at any location. Conceptually it sits alongside the other page-level logic, watching for changes to the selected policy or URL parameters and updating them accordingly, ensuring deep links open directly to the correct policy and that navigation updates the URL without disrupting the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering; it does not occupy space or influence layout flow. It operates globally on the page, synchronizing state between components as the user interacts with the interface.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The share‑link control is a compact interactive element positioned within the top region of the policy content viewer, integrated into the metadata banner area. It sits near the top‑right corner of the metadata banner, horizontally aligned with the policy title and other metadata elements. Visually, it might be a simple text link (e.g., “Copy link”) or a small icon plus label, occupying only a small footprint (roughly the size of a button with 8–12px internal padding and minimal text). A margin of about 16–24px from the right edge of the content column and 8–12px from the top of the metadata banner keeps it from feeling cramped against the borders. Its styling is understated—using a muted accent color and subtle hover states—ensuring it is discoverable when looked for but does not overpower the title or the main body text. On smaller screens, it can drop beneath the title within the same metadata banner, aligned to the right or baseline-aligned with secondary text, while maintaining at least 8–12px vertical separation from other elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned relative within the metadata banner container, anchored to its top-right area. It scrolls together with the policy content viewer. On narrow viewports, it reflows within the metadata banner, possibly moving to a new line to maintain readability and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy metadata banner appears at the very top of the policy content viewer’s right column, acting like the form’s header area in the mockup. It spans the full width of the right column, with horizontal padding of about 24–32px and vertical padding of 16–24px, creating a distinct yet subtle band. Its top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the policy list sidebar, creating a clear visual baseline across the two columns. Within this banner, the policy title is left‑aligned and prominent, set in dark text with a larger type size, while secondary metadata (such as category or last updated date) appears below or to the right in a smaller, muted style. The share‑link control sits in the top‑right portion of this banner, balancing the composition without crowding the title. The banner’s background may be slightly differentiated (e.g., a very light gray tint or a faint bottom border) from the pure white of the main content area to define it as a header strip without drawing too much attention. A generous gap of approximately 24–32px separates the bottom of the banner from the first line of the policy body text below, ensuring a clear transition from structural information to reading content. On scroll, the banner may either scroll away with the content or, if desired, take on a subtle sticky behavior within the right column so that the policy title and share control remain visible as the user reads further down.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered as the first child of the policy content viewer, occupying full width within that column and flowing with the document. Optionally, it can adopt a sticky position within the right column with a top offset just below the global header, but it remains confined to the horizontal bounds of the content viewer. On smaller screens, it remains at the top of the stacked content and scrolls normally.
